turns.content: I can't decide between Cherry MX Brown, Gateron Yellow, and Holy Pandas. What's the actual feel difference?

Cherry MX Brown: light tactile with a small, rounded bump and relatively low tactility; many people describe it as “scratchy” stock because Cherry’s housing and stem tolerances plus factory lube (or lack of it) can feel dry. The bump is subtle and occurs early in the keypress. It’s a “do-everything” switch but doesn’t strongly commit to either tactile or linear. Gateron Yellow: linear, typically medium weight (commonly ~50g actuation-ish, ~63g bottom-out depending on variant). Stock feel is often smoother than Cherry Browns because Gateron’s molds and plastic blend tend to glide better. No bump—just a straight travel. People like them for a “creamy” or “buttery” linear when lubed, especially the milky or KS-3 variants. Holy Panda: strong tactile. Big, sharp-ish bump with a pronounced “event” near the top. The tactility is much higher than Browns—more of a “pop” than a “nudge.” They’re usually higher spring weight (bottom-out often in the upper 60s to 70g range). Stock can be decent, but many people still lube carefully (avoiding the legs if they want to keep maximum tactility). Sound tends to be deeper/thockier than Browns, but depends heavily on board, plate, and keycaps. If you want: Browns = subtle tactile; Yellows = smooth linear; Holy Pandas = aggressive tactile. If you tell me what you’re optimizing for (typing feel, gaming, sound profile, fatigue, etc.), I can narrow it down.
Optimize for typing feel and sound, not gaming. I like “thock” but also don’t want it to be too loud. I’m not sure if I want tactile or linear.

For typing feel + controlled “thock,” you can get there with either tactile or linear, but the path differs: 1) If you want tactile: - Holy Pandas can be very satisfying for typing because the bump gives clear feedback. But they can be louder because the bump encourages a more decisive press and bottom-out, plus many Panda-style housings have a deeper “clack-thock” combo depending on plate. - If you want less loud but still tactile, Browns are quieter-ish in theory due to weaker bump, but their sound can be plasticky and higher-pitched unless the build is tuned. 2) If you want linear: - Gateron Yellows (especially milky top/bottom or KS-3) can produce a deep sound when paired with a softer mounting style, thicker keycaps, and some case dampening. Linears often become “thocky” when lubed and filmed, and when you control bottom-out with plate material and maybe slightly heavier springs. To keep “thock” but not too loud: - Use a deeper-sounding switch (often nylon bottoms, POM stems, etc.). - Use thicker keycaps (PBT thick, or ABS doubleshot can be deeper depending on profile). - Consider plate choice: polycarbonate (PC) and FR4 can soften and deepen; brass and aluminum can be louder/brighter. - Foam and/or silicone in the case can reduce hollowness (not always “thock,” but reduces echo). - Lubing + filming reduces scratch and can lower pitch, but over-lubing can make it muted. Decision shortcut: - If you want a “typing event” and like feeling the actuation: lean Holy Panda (or other strong tactile). - If you want a smooth, deep, less “clicky” experience and can rely on sound/weight for satisfaction: lean Gateron Yellow. What board are you building (layout, mounting style), and what plate/keycap material are you considering? That’s a huge part of sound.
Assume a generic 65% kit, gasket mount, polycarbonate plate, and thick PBT caps. Hot-swap PCB. I’m trying to avoid too much modding but I’ll lube if it’s worth it.

With gasket mount + PC plate + thick PBT, you’re already steering toward a softer, lower-pitched sound. In that environment: - Gateron Yellow: very safe pick. On PC plate in a gasket mount, lubed Yellows can be deep and not overly loud. They’ll feel smooth and slightly “bouncy” because PC plate flex + gaskets. Minimal modding: a light lube job (205g0 on stems/housings, 105 on springs) is usually enough. Films optional depending on housing tolerance; many Yellows are fine without. - Cherry MX Brown: they can work, but stock Cherry Browns in a PC plate gasket build may still feel scratchy and the bump can feel vague. If you don’t want heavy modding, Browns are the least “wow” option. They also can sound a bit higher-pitched/plasticky compared to Yellows in many builds. Lubing Browns helps a lot, but you have to decide whether to lube legs (reduces tactility) or avoid legs (keeps bump but may keep some scratch). If you’re doing the work anyway, there are better tactiles than Browns at similar effort. - Holy Panda: in that build, they’ll feel very tactile and can sound satisfyingly deep with PBT and PC plate. They can still be louder than linears because the tactile event + bottom-out. If you type with a lighter touch and don’t slam keys, they can be controlled. Minimal modding: careful lube (light on rails, avoid legs if you want full bump), maybe spring lube. Films sometimes help consistency depending on the specific Panda version. If you want minimal modding and a reliable “thock but not loud,” I’d pick Gateron Yellow and lube them. If you strongly prefer tactile feedback for typing, Holy Pandas (light lube, no leg lube) are the tactile route—just accept they may be a bit more assertive in sound. Do you know if you prefer long springs/heavier bottom-out, or do you get finger fatigue easily?

I don’t know about spring preferences. I’ve used laptop keyboards and a basic prebuilt mechanical with Reds. Reds felt kind of mushy and I bottomed out a lot. I want something more “solid” but not exhausting.

Coming from laptop + MX Reds: - Reds feeling “mushy” is often: light spring + linear + you bottom out hard, so the bottom-out dominates the feel. “Solid” usually comes from either (a) more weight, (b) a tactile event that encourages you not to bottom out as hard, (c) a firmer bottom-out feel from switch/plate/case, or some combo. Options within your three: 1) Gateron Yellow: compared to Reds, Yellows are typically a bit heavier. That extra weight can make the downstroke feel more controlled and “solid,” and can reduce accidental presses. You may still bottom out, but it can feel less like you’re falling through the key travel. In a gasket/PC build, it’ll be soft-ish though; if you want “solid,” you might prefer a slightly stiffer plate (FR4) or less flexy gasket implementation—but you can still get a satisfying feel with PC. 2) Holy Panda: the tactile bump gives you a “structure” in the press, so it can feel solid even if you bottom out. Some people actually bottom out less because the bump provides feedback. But they’re heavier, and if you’re used to light keys, they can be tiring at first. Many people adapt quickly for typing. 3) Cherry Brown: tactile exists but may not solve the “mushy” feeling because the bump is mild. You might still bottom out similarly, and the switch doesn’t give that “solid event” like a strong tactile does. Given your description, the two likely winners are: - Gateron Yellow (lubed) if you want linear but more controlled than Reds. - Holy Panda if you want a strong tactile to make typing feel more deliberate and structured. A practical approach: buy a small batch (10–20) of each and put them in common keys for a week: alphas, space, enter, backspace. Hot-swap makes this easy. That will tell you more than any description. If you had to choose: do you like the idea of feeling a bump, or do you want the keypress to be uninterrupted?

I think I like the idea of a bump, but I’m worried Holy Pandas will be too much. Are there differences among “Holy Panda” versions? And how do they compare to Browns tactility-wise, like quantitatively?

Yes—“Holy Panda” isn’t one perfectly standardized switch across all vendors, and versions differ in feel, sound, and consistency. Common Holy Panda variants: - Drop Holy Panda / Invyr: widely known, strong tactile, generally consistent, but can vary batch-to-batch. Sound is often deep and “poppy.” - Glorious Panda: similar concept, sometimes slightly different pitch and leaf feel; can have spring differences; many find them a bit less “refined” stock but good lubed. - BSUN/YOK Panda-derived “Holy Panda” style: there are many Panda housings used with different stems; some are closer to the “classic” feel than others. Why they differ: - Stem geometry: the tactile bump comes from the shape of the tactile legs interacting with the leaf. Small differences in the ramp angle and leg length change sharpness and peak force. - Leaf: leaf stiffness and finish affects tactility and scratch. - Housing materials: nylon vs polycarbonate tops, etc., affect sound. - Spring: weight and length affect perceived tactility and return. Quantitative comparison is tricky without force curves from the exact variants, but conceptually: - Cherry Brown tactility is low. People often describe it as ~“light tactile” where the bump is small and smooth. - Holy Panda is “high tactile.” Peak force is much higher relative to the start force, and the bump is more concentrated near the top. If we talk in broad categories: - Browns: gentle bump; many can barely feel it if typing fast. - Holy Pandas: unmistakable bump; you’ll notice it on every keypress. If you want a bump but fear Holy Panda is too much, there’s a whole middle ground of “medium tactiles” (e.g., Durock T1 / Koala, Boba U4T, etc.)—but staying within your named options, Holy Panda is the only one that really delivers a strong tactile experience. A compromise within the Holy Panda world: - Lube lightly (rails only, no leg lube) to keep bump but reduce scratch. - Consider a slightly lighter spring swap (e.g., 62g) if the stock is heavy. That can reduce fatigue while keeping tactility shape. Do you prefer a “rounded bump” (smooth hill) or a “sharp bump” (more sudden drop-off)? Holy Pandas are closer to sharp/pop.
Rounded bump sounds nicer. Browns are rounded, right? But people complain they’re scratchy and not very tactile. Is there a way to make Browns better without turning them into something else?

Browns are generally a rounded, mild bump—yes. Making them “better” without fundamentally changing them is mostly about smoothness, sound, and consistency rather than increasing tactility dramatically. Ways to improve Cherry MX Browns while keeping their character: 1) Lube carefully - Use a lighter lube than you would on linears if you want to preserve tactility. Many people use something like Tribosys 3203/3204 on tactiles; 205g0 can work but is easier to overdo. - Apply thinly on stem rails and housing rails. Avoid the tactile legs if you want to keep the bump as-is. - Lube springs (105 oil or bag lube) to eliminate ping. 2) Break-in - Cherry switches can smooth out after use. You can accelerate by actuating them a lot, but real use also works. 3) Films (optional) - Cherry housings can have some wobble; films can tighten sound and reduce rattle. However, many Cherry tops/bottoms don’t accept all film types equally; you need the right thickness. Overly tight films can cause binding. 4) Stabilizers + keycaps + plate - A lot of perceived “scratch” and “rattle” comes from stabs and keycap wobble. Good stab tuning (clipped if needed, lubed, balanced) makes the whole board feel more premium even if the switch is modest. - Thick PBT already helps. What you can’t easily do without “turning them into something else”: - You can’t make Browns into a strong tactile without swapping stems (which becomes a frankenswitch) or moving to a different tactile switch design. If your goal is rounded bump + not loud + pleasant sound, a well-lubed Brown build can be nice, just not “exciting.” If you want more tactile without going sharp, you’d normally pick a medium tactile with a rounder bump profile. Given you’re hot-swap and trying not to mod heavily, do you want a switch that’s good stock, or are you okay with spending a few hours lubing?

I’m okay spending a few hours lubing, but I don’t want it to be a never-ending tweak project. Also, I keep hearing about “leaf ping” and “spring ping.” Which of these switches are more prone to weird noises?

“Spring ping” is resonance from the spring vibrating; “leaf ping” is from the metal leaf (contact) resonating. Both can be influenced by case acoustics. Typical tendencies: - Cherry MX Brown: can have spring ping stock; leaf noise varies. Cherry springs can be a bit “ringy.” Lubing springs usually solves most of it. Leaf ping is less common than spring ping but can happen. - Gateron Yellow: depends on variant and batch. Many are pretty quiet spring-wise, but some can have spring crunch/ping. Again, spring lube is the main fix. Leaf ping is generally not a hallmark issue for Yellows, but any switch can have it occasionally. - Holy Panda: sometimes more leaf noise because the tactile interaction is more forceful and can excite the leaf. Also, the strong bump can make any metallic resonance more noticeable. Spring ping can happen too. Noise control hierarchy: 1) Lube springs (biggest bang-for-buck). 2) Make sure the switch is seated well in the plate/PCB. 3) Case dampening to reduce amplification. 4) For leaf ping specifically: sometimes a tiny amount of lube placed carefully near the leaf contact area can reduce it, but you must be careful not to contaminate the contact surfaces and cause chatter. Many people avoid lubing leaf directly unless they know what they’re doing. If you want a “set it and forget it” path: - Lube springs for whichever switch you pick. - For linears (Yellows), lube stems/housings moderately. - For tactiles (Browns/Pandas), lube lightly and avoid legs unless you explicitly want to soften tactility. Do you care more about a deep sound or a clean, crisp sound? “Thock” can sometimes come with a slightly muted character.
Deep sound, but not mushy. Crisp is okay as long as it doesn’t become high-pitched. Also, what about long-pole stems? I see people talk about long-pole “clack.” Do any of these have that?

Long-pole stems reduce total travel before bottom-out because the stem pole hits the bottom housing sooner. That often produces: - A sharper, more percussive bottom-out (“clack”), sometimes higher in pitch. - Less dependence on the keycap hitting the top housing (because it bottoms earlier). - A different feel: slightly shorter travel and a more immediate bottom-out. Among your three: - Classic Cherry MX Brown: not typically long-pole. - Gateron Yellow (standard): not typically long-pole. Some newer “pro” or special editions might have different stem lengths, but the common Yellow is standard. - Holy Panda: many Panda-style stems are not what people call “long-pole” in the modern sense, but they can still have a punchy bottom-out due to housing/stem geometry. Some Holy Panda-like variants or “Panda” derivatives can be closer to that snappy bottom-out, but it’s not the same as the explicitly long-pole linears/tactiles popular now. If you want deep but not mushy: - Avoid super-soft bottom-out setups (very flexible plate + very soft gaskets + thin caps) or super-heavy lube that dampens too much. - Thick PBT can sometimes sound a bit “marbly” and muted; that’s often good for deep sound. - PC plate + gasket can be deep, but if it’s too soft, you can regain crispness with slightly stiffer gaskets, less foam, or a different plate (FR4 is a good “deep but crisp” compromise). Switch choice impact: - Yellows lubed: deep and clean, can be crisp if not over-lubed. - Holy Pandas: deep and tactile “pop,” can be louder and more percussive. - Browns: can be relatively crisp but might sound thinner unless the build is tuned. If you’re curious about long-pole behavior without changing switches, you can’t really “add” long-pole. That’s a switch design choice. Do you plan to use O-rings or any kind of dampeners? Those can reduce loudness but can also feel mushy.
No O-rings. I hate the mushy feel. I’d rather tune with plate/foam/lube. Speaking of foam: does foam always make things quieter and lower pitch? Some people say it kills “thock.”

Foam often reduces hollowness and volume, but it doesn’t universally lower pitch, and it can absolutely “kill” some of the character depending on where it’s placed and how dense it is. Common foam types and effects: 1) Case foam (between PCB and bottom case, or filling the case) - Reduces cavity resonance (“echo,” “ping,” “hollow” sound). - Often makes the sound more focused and sometimes lower in perceived pitch because it removes higher-frequency reflections, but it can also make the board sound “closed-in” or muted if overdone. 2) PCB foam / plate foam (between plate and PCB) - Can reduce high-frequency clack and tighten the sound. - Can reduce “airiness.” Some people feel it makes the board less lively. - With gasket + PC plate, adding plate foam can push it toward muted/marbly. That can be good if you want quiet, but if you want crispness, you might skip it. 3) Switch pads / PE foam (PE sheet mod) - PE foam can add a brighter, “poppy” sound (sometimes called “marbly pop”) rather than simply quieting. It can increase perceived higher frequencies. - Not always “thock”; often more “pop.” So foam isn’t a one-way “lower pitch” lever. It’s more like resonance shaping. If your target is deep, controlled, not mushy: - Start with minimal foam: maybe just thin case foam or a silicone dampener. - Skip plate foam initially; you can add later if it’s too loud or too sharp. - Tune stabilizers well; stabs are often the loudest offenders. - Don’t over-lube. Given that, which switch direction sounds more appealing: smooth linear depth (Yellows) or tactile pop depth (Holy Pandas)?
Still torn. Could you describe the “tactile pop” vs “linear depth” in a way that relates to typing rhythm? Like what changes in how you type?

Linear depth (e.g., lubed Yellows): - Rhythm tends to be more even and flowing because there’s no tactile interruption. If you type fast, your fingers can “glide” through presses. - You often bottom out more consistently unless you’ve trained to float. The sound becomes a steady series of bottom-outs. - The satisfaction comes from smoothness + sound + keycap/plate feel, not from a discrete tactile event. - Mistypes: with a slightly heavier linear like Yellow, accidental presses are reduced compared to very light linears, but there’s still no tactile confirmation. Tactile pop (e.g., Holy Pandas): - Rhythm becomes more “articulated.” Each keystroke has a noticeable event near the top, so it can feel like typing has punctuation. - Some people naturally lighten their bottom-out because the bump provides feedback; others still bottom out but feel the bump first. - Sound can have two components: the tactile event (a subtle “tick/pop”) and the bottom-out/upstroke. In strong tactiles, that “pop” can be audible or at least felt. - Mistypes: the bump can give you a sense of actuation, which some people feel helps accuracy. In practice: - If you like a smooth, continuous cadence and want the keyboard to feel like an instrument with consistent notes: linear. - If you like each keypress to feel like it has a “click” without being clicky: strong tactile. Since you disliked Reds as mushy, a heavier linear may fix that. But if what you really wanted was definition, tactile does that. One hybrid approach: choose Yellows for alphas and a tactile for a few keys? Most people don’t mix like that, but with hot-swap you can experiment with just a handful of Holy Pandas on home row keys to see if it changes your typing behavior in a good way. Do you care about key wobble and stability? Holy Panda housings can feel more “tight” depending on brand; Cherry can have more wobble.
I care somewhat, but not obsessive. More important is consistency across the board. I also hear about “factory lubed” switches like Gateron Pro. Are factory-lubed Yellows good enough to skip lubing?

Factory lube has improved, but “good enough” depends on your tolerance for inconsistency. Factory-lubed Gateron Yellows (often called Gateron Pro Yellow, KS-9, etc.): Pros: - Usually smoother than completely dry switches out of the box. - Often reduces spring ping somewhat. - Saves a lot of time. Cons: - Lube amount can vary switch-to-switch: some feel great, some feel slightly sluggish or uneven. - Sometimes the lube is applied in a way that doesn’t fully address spring noise (springs may still ping). - If over-lubed, sound can become dull and feel can be slightly “sticky.” If you want consistency and you’re willing to spend a few hours once: - Buying factory-lubed and then “tuning” only the worst offenders is an option: assemble, test, pull the noisy/scratchy ones, lube those. - Or buy unlubed and lube all for maximum uniformity. For Holy Pandas, “factory-lubed” is less common in the same way, and many prefer to lube themselves anyway due to how tactility feels with lube. For Cherry Browns, factory lube generally isn’t a big selling point; many come relatively dry. If you want minimal work with good results: factory-lubed Pro Yellows are a strong candidate. You can always lube springs later if ping shows up. Do you have a preference for keypress weight? If Reds were too light, do you want “medium” or “heavier than medium”?
Medium. I don’t want to fight the keyboard. Also, can you explain actuation force vs bottom-out force? People quote numbers but I don’t know what I should care about.

Actuation force: the force required at the point where the switch registers a keypress (electrically). Bottom-out force: the force required to press the key all the way down until it stops. Why it matters: - If actuation is low but bottom-out is high (progressive spring), you can trigger keys easily but still get resistance near the end—can reduce accidental presses while still feeling supportive. - If actuation and bottom-out are both low (light spring), the switch feels easy and fast but can feel uncontrolled/mushy if you bottom out hard. - If both are high, it feels heavy and can fatigue you. For typing comfort, many people care more about bottom-out because most typists bottom out at least some of the time. A switch can have a moderate actuation but still feel heavy if bottom-out is high. Also, tactiles complicate it: - A tactile bump creates a peak force that can be higher than actuation. So you might see a “tactile peak” number. That peak is what your finger feels as the bump. Practical guidance: - If you bottom out a lot and dislike mushy: choose a switch with a slightly higher bottom-out (or a progressive spring) so you get support. - If you want less fatigue: avoid very high tactile peaks or very heavy bottom-out. In your list: - Gateron Yellow usually lands in a comfortable medium zone. - Holy Panda often has a higher tactile peak and higher bottom-out than Yellows; more “event,” potentially more fatigue. - Cherry Brown is lighter and less tactile; might not feel as solid. If you want medium and “solid,” Yellows are likely the easiest fit, unless you discover you love tactility. Do you want to prioritize a clean upstroke (less “clack” on return)? That can differ by switch and lube too.
Yes, I don’t want a loud upstroke. I notice on some boards the return is louder than the press. What controls upstroke noise?

Upstroke noise is influenced by: 1) Switch design: how the stem hits the top housing on return. Some housings/stems produce a sharper top-out. 2) Spring energy: heavier/longer springs can snap the key back faster, increasing top-out noise if you’re not controlling finger release. 3) Lubing: lube on the stem rails and inside the top housing contact points can soften top-out slightly. 4) Keycap/plate resonance: a resonant plate/case amplifies the top-out. 5) Typing style: if you fully release quickly, the switch tops out harder. Comparing your options: - Linears like Yellows: can have noticeable top-out if springs are lively and if the build is resonant. Lubing helps. Some people choose slightly slower springs or add a tiny bit more lube to calm it. - Holy Pandas: the tactile leaf interaction can add some extra “tick” if not tuned, and return can be snappy. But many Panda housings sound deep, and lube can help. - Cherry Browns: can have a higher-pitched top-out and some spring noise; again, lube helps. If upstroke noise is a concern and you want “thock not clack,” you can: - Lube properly (stems + springs). - Avoid overly stiff plates. - Use a bit of case dampening. - Consider slightly heavier keycaps (thick PBT helps). - Consider a spring with less “bounciness” (some springs are more resonant than others). You can also do a quick test when you get switches: hold a single switch between fingers and press/release near your ear. Spring ping and top-out character become obvious. Are you planning on using screw-in stabilizers? Stab tuning affects perceived loudness a lot.
Yes, screw-in stabs. I’ll lube them. On switches: if I buy Yellows, which exact variant should I look for? There are milky, black bottom, clear top, etc.

Common Gateron Yellow variants and typical traits: 1) Gateron KS-3 Yellow (often “black bottom, clear top”) - Classic, widely available. - Generally smooth, consistent. - Sound: fairly balanced; can be a bit clacky depending on build, but lubing + your PC plate/PBT will deepen it. 2) “Milky” Gateron Yellow (milky top and/or milky bottom) - Milkier nylon blend tends to deepen sound and soften the tone. - Often described as more “thocky” than clear-top variants. - Sometimes slightly less smooth stock than clear-top, but lubing makes them excellent. 3) Gateron Yellow Pro (factory-lubed) - Convenience + decent stock smoothness. - Sound can be a little more muted due to factory lube. - Consistency varies by batch. If your goal is deep, controlled, not too loud: - Milky Yellows are a strong choice for sound. - If you want maximum smoothness and don’t mind a slightly brighter tone: KS-3 clears. - If you want minimal work: Yellow Pros. Given you’re okay with lubing: I’d go Milky (at least milky bottom) and do a clean lube job. Milky bottom + clear top is a nice compromise: deeper bottom-out tone with a bit of clarity. Do you care about RGB shine-through? Milky tops reduce light transmission.
No RGB priority. So milky is fine. If I go Holy Pandas, what should I watch out for? Any common problems like chatter on hot-swap?

For Holy Pandas (and many strong tactiles) in a hot-swap build, watch for: 1) Pin alignment and bending - Panda-style switches often have slightly thicker pins or tighter tolerances; be careful inserting into hot-swap sockets to avoid bending pins or damaging sockets. 2) Contact leaf sensitivity / chatter - Chatter can happen if tolerances are off or if the contact leaf is contaminated. Avoid lubing near the leaf/contact area. Keep everything clean. - Some batches from some vendors have more reports than others. It’s not guaranteed, but it’s something to be aware of. 3) Consistency - Some Holy Panda batches have variance in tactility/sound. Buying from a reputable vendor with recent stock helps. 4) Tactile “tick” - Strong tactiles can develop a faint tick (often leaf-related) that’s more noticeable than on linears. Sometimes it’s there stock; sometimes lubing fixes it; sometimes it persists. 5) Lubing difficulty - You must be more careful than with linears. Too much lube can make tactiles feel sluggish or reduce the crispness of the bump. - Avoid lube on legs if you want full tactility; if you do lube legs, do it extremely lightly and accept a softened bump. 6) Spring weight - If the spring is heavy and you’re used to light boards, you might need an adjustment period. Hot-swap compatibility is usually fine if you’re careful. If you’re worried, you can insert switches by supporting the socket area from below (if the case is open) and ensuring pins are straight. If you want a low-risk first build, Yellows are simpler. If you want a tactile “signature,” Holy Pandas are more distinctive but higher risk in terms of preference. Do you have a switch opener and basic tools, or would you be starting from scratch?
Starting from scratch. I can buy tools. What do I actually need for lubing switches properly, and how long does it take for a 65%?

Basic switch lubing kit: - Switch opener (compatible with MX-style; some open both top styles). - Fine brush (size 00 or similar). - Lube: for linears, Krytox 205g0 is common; for tactiles, Tribosys 3203/3204 or very light 205g0. For springs, Krytox 105 oil or bag-lube with 105/205 mix. - Switch films (optional; 0.15mm/0.125mm depending on switch). - Tweezers (to handle springs/films). - Small containers or a lube station (nice but optional). - Gloves optional; paper towels for cleanup. Time estimate for a 65% (usually 67–68 switches): - First time, linears: 3–6 hours depending on pace and whether you film. - Tactiles: similar time, but you may go slower to avoid over-lubing. - Stabilizers: add 30–90 minutes depending on experience. Workflow tips: - Do a test: lube 3–5 switches first, install them temporarily, type a bit, adjust technique if they feel too sluggish or too dry. - Keep lube application consistent; the main goal is uniformity. - Springs: bag-lube is efficient (put springs in a bag with a few drops of oil, shake, let sit). If you want to minimize tools/time: factory-lubed Yellow Pros + just lube stabs is the lowest effort decent result. Are you aiming for a particular sound descriptor—like “marbly,” “creamy,” “muted thock,” “clean thock”?
“Clean thock.” Not marbly pop. I don’t want that PE foam sound. Clean, deep, not overly muted.

For “clean thock”: - Skip PE foam. - Use minimal-to-moderate case dampening (thin foam or silicone) to remove hollowness, not to mute everything. - PC plate is fine; FR4 can be even “cleaner” sometimes, but PC can still do it. - Thick PBT is good; choose a profile that doesn’t get too sharp (some tall profiles can sound different). Switch choice: - Lubed milky Gateron Yellows are a classic clean-thock path: smooth, deep, controlled. - Holy Pandas can be clean-thock too, but tactiles often add a bit of texture/presence that can read as “clackier” if the bump/top-out is pronounced. Lube approach for clean: - Don’t overlube. Overlubing makes it muted and can reduce clarity. - For Yellows: light 205g0 on rails + a touch on stem sides; avoid flooding. - Springs: light oil to avoid ping without making return sluggish. Also, stabilizers matter hugely for clean thock: - Use a thicker lube (dielectric grease or 205g0) on stab wires where they contact housing. - Balance wires so they don’t tick. - Consider adding a small amount of padding under stab housings (some do “pads”)—but keep it minimal to avoid mush. If you’re leaning linear, that setup will likely hit your target with minimal drama. Do you want to consider a slightly heavier spring in Yellows, or stick with stock?
Stick with stock for now. Another question: how does switch “scratchiness” relate to materials? People say POM stems, nylon housings, etc. Can you explain what actually causes scratch?

Scratchiness is friction irregularity during travel. Causes include: 1) Surface finish and tooling marks - The stem rails sliding against housing rails can have microscopic roughness. Some manufacturers have smoother molds/polish than others. 2) Material pairing - Common materials: POM stems, nylon housings, polycarbonate tops. - POM is naturally low-friction, often smooth. - Nylon can be smooth but can also feel “dry” depending on finish. - Polycarbonate tops can change sound more than feel, but tolerances matter. 3) Tolerances and wobble - If the stem wobbles, it can rub unevenly on rails, making scratch more noticeable. 4) Lack of lubrication - Dry plastic-on-plastic can feel scratchy even if surfaces are decent. 5) Leaf interaction (especially tactiles) - In tactiles, the tactile leg rubs the leaf, which can feel like scratch if the contact surfaces are rough or dry. That’s separate from rail scratch. Why Cherry Browns are often called scratchy: - Cherry’s classic molds can feel dry and have noticeable friction stock compared to many modern budget linears. - Their tactility is mild, so the scratch can be more noticeable than the bump. Why Gateron Yellows are often called smoother: - Generally smoother rails stock, and as linears they don’t have tactile leg/leaf friction. Why Holy Pandas can feel “textured”: - The tactile leg/leaf interaction is intense; even if rails are smooth, you can feel leaf friction. Lubing reduces scratch by filling microscopic irregularities and smoothing motion. Films can reduce wobble-related scratch by stabilizing the top housing. If scratch is your main fear, linears like Yellows are easiest to make buttery smooth. Do you prefer a “dry” clean sound or a “creamy” sound? People use those terms loosely, but it can reflect how damped the switch feels.
Probably closer to “dry clean” than creamy. I like clarity. But still deep. Is that contradictory?

Not necessarily. “Deep” is mostly pitch; “dry/clean” is about resonance and lack of reverb or mushy over-damping. You can get deep + clean by: - Using materials that naturally lower pitch (nylon bottoms, thick caps, a case that isn’t too resonant). - Avoiding mods that add artificial “pop” (PE foam) or over-dampen (too much foam, too much lube). - Ensuring tight, consistent assembly (no rattles, no loose parts). Switch-wise: - Milky Yellows can be deep; if you lube lightly and don’t foam everything, they can stay clean. - If milky feels too “soft,” KS-3 clear-top Yellows can be a bit clearer in sound while still deepened by your board choices. Another lever: keycap profile and thickness. Some thick PBT sets are very deep but slightly muted. If you want clarity, choose a set known for a cleaner tone (still thick, but not overly damp). Also, consistent keycap fit reduces ticking. Do you want to consider stabilizer type (e.g., TX, Durock, Owlstabs) or just “any screw-in”?
I’ll pick a decent set like TX. Back to switches: if I buy 90 switches, how do I sort out inconsistent ones? Is there a method?

Yes—basic QC sorting helps a lot: 1) Physical inspection - Check pins are straight and plating looks uniform. - Check housings for cracks, bent leaves (rare but possible). 2) Hand-feel test (before lubing) - Press each switch a few times in-hand. - Flag any that feel unusually scratchy, crunchy, or have a weird tactile tick (for tactiles). 3) Sound test (optional but useful) - Use a simple switch tester plate or even mount a few in the PCB temporarily. - Listen for spring ping or leaf tick. 4) Spring sorting (advanced) - If you’re very particular, you can weigh springs or at least separate any that feel significantly heavier/lighter. Most people skip this. 5) Post-lube retest - After lubing, test again. Sometimes a switch will feel sluggish if it got too much lube; you can wipe and redo. Practical approach: - For a 65%, buy 10–20 extra. - Before lubing, pick the best-feeling ones for alphas (most-used keys). - Reserve slightly noisier ones for less-used keys or as spares. Consistency is easier with linears; tactiles amplify small differences. Do you plan on using a specific key layout (split backspace, stepped caps, etc.) that might require additional stabilizers or odd key sizes?

Standard 65%. Nothing exotic. I think I’m leaning milky Gateron Yellows now. But I’m worried I’ll miss tactile feedback. Is there a way to simulate tactility with linears? Like heavier springs or something?

You can’t truly simulate a tactile bump with a linear—there’s no force discontinuity. But you can get some of the benefits people seek from tactility: - Heavier springs: give more resistance and control, reducing accidental presses and encouraging lighter bottom-out control. It feels more “intentional,” but still smooth. - Progressive springs: increase resistance near bottom, which can feel supportive and reduce harsh bottom-out. Still linear, but the force curve changes. - Shorter travel / long-pole linears: create a more immediate bottom-out, which some interpret as “feedback,” but it’s bottom-out feedback, not actuation feedback. - Sound/plate feel: a crisp, clean bottom-out can provide a satisfying sense of completion. If you want real tactile feedback, you need a tactile switch. Another compromise: choose a mild-to-medium tactile rather than Holy Panda. But if you’re set on Yellows, you can still build a very satisfying typing board—many typists prefer linears once they’re smooth and well-tuned. Since you’re hot-swap, you can also start with Yellows and later swap in tactiles without rebuilding the whole board. Would you want to keep a small set of tactiles (like 10) to experiment on home row later?
That’s a good idea. If I do that, should I pick Browns or Holy Pandas for the sample? Or something else?

Between Browns and Holy Pandas as a “tactile sample,” I’d pick Holy Pandas if the goal is to clearly understand whether you like tactility. Browns can be so mild that you might conclude “tactiles do nothing for me,” when really you just tried a very light tactile. But if you specifically want to test a rounded, gentle tactile: Browns are that, but they might not feel dramatically different from a linear for some people. If “something else” is allowed, a medium tactile with a rounded bump would be ideal as a reference point. But sticking to your three: buy a small batch of Holy Pandas, and maybe 5 Browns if you’re curious. Suggested experiment: - Install Yellows everywhere. - Put Holy Pandas on F and J (home keys) and maybe Enter/Backspace for a day. - Notice if your typing accuracy/comfort improves or if it feels disruptive/loud. - If it’s too much, you’ve learned you prefer linears or mild tactiles. Be aware: mixing switches changes sound across keys, so it’s more about feel testing than final aesthetics. Do you have a preference for switch top material (milky vs clear) in terms of sound? Clear tops can sound slightly brighter.
I’ll go milky. Okay, last big thing: lubing technique. For milky Yellows, where exactly do I apply lube, and how do I avoid overdoing it?

For milky Gateron Yellows (linear), a controlled, light 205g0 application is a common approach: Where to lube: 1) Stem - Lube the two side rails (the long flat surfaces that slide in the housing rails). - Lightly lube the front/back of the stem where it contacts the housing guides (don’t cake it). - You can add a tiny amount on the stem pole sides, but avoid putting a blob on the very bottom of the pole; that can affect sound/feel and migrate. 2) Bottom housing - Lube the two rails where the stem slides. - Lightly lube the circular area where the spring sits (optional; mostly for smoothing sound). - Avoid the metal leaf/contact area entirely. 3) Top housing - Usually not necessary to lube much. Some people add a tiny amount to areas where the stem might contact on return to soften top-out, but it’s easy to overdo. 4) Springs - Best bang-for-buck: bag-lube with a few drops of Krytox 105 oil (or very thin lube). Put springs in a small bag, add a few drops, shake until lightly coated. - Or donut-dip each spring end in 205g0 (slower, but very controlled). How to avoid overdoing it: - Use a small amount on the brush; wipe excess on the jar rim. - You should barely see lube; it should look like a thin sheen. - Do 3 switches, reassemble, test in PCB. If they feel sluggish or sound too muted, reduce lube. - Consistency matters more than “perfect coverage.” Films: - Try without films first. If you notice housing wobble or inconsistent sound, add films. Milky housings sometimes benefit, but it’s not mandatory. If you want “clean” sound: go lighter rather than heavier on lube. How sensitive are you to a slightly slower return? Some people notice it; others don’t.
I’m sensitive to sluggishness. I want a snappy return. Does lubing inevitably slow return, or is that mostly over-lubing? And do milky housings feel slower than clear?

Lubing doesn’t inherently make return sluggish if applied lightly and correctly; sluggishness is mostly from over-lubing or using a very thick lube in places that create hydraulic drag. What causes sluggish return: - Too much thick lube on stem rails or inside housing rails. - Lube getting into tight spots where it can “pool.” - Over-lubed springs can also feel slightly damped, but usually spring oiling is fine. How to keep it snappy: - Use a thin, even coat on rails only. - Avoid lubing the stem pole heavily. - Consider using a slightly lighter lube (or lighter application) if you’re very sensitive. Some people prefer 205g0 but applied extremely thin; others use a slightly lighter tactile lube even on linears for speed (with trade-offs). Milky vs clear housing speed: - The housing material itself doesn’t inherently make it “slower,” but different tolerances and surface finishes can change perceived friction. Some milky variants can feel slightly more “soft” or “muted,” but once lubed, the difference is subtle. - Clear-top variants sometimes feel a hair more “crisp” sounding; again, subtle. If snappy return is a priority and you’re staying stock springs: - Don’t over-oil springs. A minimal amount is enough to stop ping. - Keep lube off the leaf area; contamination can cause issues. You can also test one switch unlubed, one lightly lubed, one medium lubed, and compare return speed in the PCB. Do you plan to use a keycap profile like Cherry, OEM, or something taller like SA? Profiles change perceived snappiness too.
Cherry profile thick PBT. Okay. If I buy milky Yellows and lube lightly, should I still film? What does filming actually do for sound/feel?

Filming inserts a thin layer (film) between top and bottom housing to reduce housing wobble and tighten the seal. Effects: Feel: - Slightly less stem wobble (though films primarily reduce housing wobble; stem wobble is a different thing). - Can make keypress feel more stable, especially if the housings are loose. Sound: - Often makes the sound more consistent and a bit deeper/cleaner because there’s less micro-rattle between housings. - Can reduce high-frequency “rattle” and make bottom-out/top-out more solid. Downsides: - Adds time and complexity. - If films are too thick for the switch tolerances, they can cause the housing to not fully close, or create binding, or make the switch hard to open/close. - Some switches already have tight housings; filming may do little. For milky Gateron Yellows: - Many people do film them, but it’s not universally necessary. - If you want to avoid a never-ending project: skip films initially. Build, listen, and if you notice inconsistency or rattle, you can film later. Hot-swap makes later changes possible, though it’s still work. A compromise: film only the noisiest or loosest switches (but then you lose uniformity). Usually it’s all-or-nothing for consistency. If you’re targeting “clean thock,” films can help, but they’re not mandatory. Are you okay with opening switches twice if you decide later to film?
I’d rather do it once. Maybe I’ll film them if it’s worth it. What film thickness and type should I get?

For Gateron-style MX housings, common film thicknesses are 0.15mm and 0.125mm. Which works best depends on housing tolerance. General guidance: - 0.15mm (e.g., Deskeys) is popular and often works well for Gateron. It tightens things noticeably. - 0.125mm can be safer if you’re worried about too-tight closure or binding, but might have a smaller effect. Film materials: - Polycarbonate films: common, consistent. - Poron/foam films: can dampen more; sometimes used to reduce high frequencies further. For “clean” sound, PC films are usually a safe default. If you want one recommendation for milky Yellows: - Try 0.15mm PC films (Deskeys-style) if you’re confident the housings aren’t already extremely tight. - If you’re cautious: 0.125mm PC films. Since you’re sensitive to sluggishness, avoid anything that could create friction/binding. Binding is rare if you use the right thickness, but it can happen if tolerances stack up. If you buy extra switches, you can test-fit films on a few before committing to all. Do you have a preference for 3-pin vs 5-pin switches? Many kits support both; 5-pin is more stable.
5-pin. The kit supports it. Great. Can we talk about stabilizer tuning briefly? I want the spacebar to match the rest of the board’s thock.

For a clean, deep, non-rattly spacebar: 1) Choose good stabs - TX screw-ins are solid. Ensure correct size: 6.25u or 7u space depending on your layout. 2) Balance the wire - Before installing, check the wire on a flat surface; it should be even. If one side lifts, you’ll get ticking. - Minor bends can be corrected carefully. 3) Lubing - Housing + stem: a medium lube like 205g0 on the stab stem sides and inside housing where it slides. - Wire: a thicker lube on the wire where it sits in the stem (dielectric grease or a thicker application of 205g0). This is where most rattle comes from. 4) Avoid over-lubing - Too much thick grease can make the spacebar feel sluggish or “stuck.” - Aim for rattle-free but still snappy return. 5) Optional: stabilizer pads - Small pads under stab housings can reduce bottom-out noise and prevent PCB wear, but can add softness. Since you dislike mush, use minimal padding or skip. 6) Spacebar keycap - Spacebars vary widely in warp and sound. Thick PBT spacebars can be deep but sometimes warped, causing uneven sound. Check for warp and consider trying a different spacebar if it sounds off. To match switch thock: - Lubed stabilizers + a good spacebar is 80% of it. - Some people add a tiny strip of foam inside the spacebar to deepen and reduce hollowness; done lightly, it doesn’t have to feel mushy. But it’s optional. Do you want a slightly louder, more present spacebar (as a “bass note”) or perfectly uniform with alphas?

Slightly more bass is fine, just no rattle. Okay. I think I have a plan: milky Yellows, light lube, maybe films, tuned TX stabs, minimal case foam, no PE. Before I commit: any reason at all to pick Cherry Browns in this build?

Reasons to pick Cherry Browns here would be mostly preference-based rather than performance-based: - If you specifically want a mild, rounded tactile and don’t want the intensity of Holy Pandas. - If you like the classic Cherry feel and are okay with “subtlety.” - If you value a lighter press than Yellows (though Browns aren’t always much lighter in bottom-out feel, and tactility can change perception). - If you want to avoid the “linear bottom-out cadence” and want some feedback without a strong bump. But in your stated goals—clean deep sound, not mushy, not too loud, minimal tweaking—Browns are hard to justify because: - Stock they can be scratchy and not very satisfying. - After lubing, they can be decent, but you’re doing work for a result that many still find “just okay.” - Sound tends to be less deep than milky Yellows in many builds. So unless you already know you like mild tactiles, Yellows are the more predictable success. If you want to keep a tactile reference, grab a small pack of Holy Pandas (or another medium tactile if you allow) as a curiosity test later. Want a shopping list with approximate quantities for a 65% so you don’t overbuy?
Yes. Include consumables like lube amounts and films.

For a standard 65% (assume 67–68 switches): Switches: - 90x milky Gateron Yellow (5-pin). Gives spares for QC and future replacements. - Optional: 10x Holy Panda (for testing) if you want. Stabilizers: - 1x TX screw-in stabilizer set appropriate for 65% (usually includes 6.25u space + 2u stabs for Enter/Backspace/Shift). Confirm your layout needs (typically 4–5x 2u plus 1x space). Films (optional): - If filming: 90x switch films. - Thickness: 0.125mm or 0.15mm PC films. If unsure, 0.125mm is safer. Lube: - Krytox 205g0: 5g jar is enough for one board (often enough for multiple). For 70–90 switches lightly lubed, 5g is plenty. - Krytox 105 oil for springs: 3–5ml is more than enough. - Stabilizer lube: either dielectric grease (small tube) or use 205g0 more heavily on wire contact points. Many use both: 205g0 for housings, dielectric grease for wires. Tools: - Switch opener (MX). - 1–2 fine brushes. - Tweezers. - Small zip bag for spring lubing. - Optional: switch lube station (nice but not required). - Keycap puller + switch puller (often included with kits). Foam: - If your kit includes case foam, start with that. If not, a thin case foam or silicone sheet cut to fit. Keep it minimal given your sound goals. Quantities: You’ll likely use: - 205g0: maybe 1–2g for a full set if you’re light-handed. - 105 oil: only a few drops for bag lubing. If you want to minimize risk, do a “pilot run” on 10 switches first before you commit to lubing all 70+. Any interest in trying different springs later (progressive vs linear), or are you aiming to keep it stock indefinitely?
Keep it stock indefinitely. This should be enough. One more: how do I evaluate the build when it’s done? Like what should I listen/feel for to diagnose issues?

Post-build evaluation checklist: Feel: - Consistency across alphas: no random scratchy keys, no noticeably heavier/lighter keys. - Return: keys should rebound quickly without feeling sticky. - Wobble: some is normal, but check for any keys that feel unusually loose (could be an unseated switch or loose plate fit). Sound: - Listen for spring ping: a metallic ring after keypress. If present, identify keys and consider relubing springs on those switches. - Listen for leaf tick: a faint metallic tick during press/release. Sometimes it’s switch-specific; swapping that switch out is easiest. - Listen for hollowness: case resonance. If it’s too hollow, add thin case dampening; if it’s too muted, remove foam. - Stabilizers: check for rattle on space/enter/backspace/shift. If ticking on one side, wire balance or wire lube is the fix. Mechanical issues: - Unregistered keys or chatter: could be a bent pin, mis-seated switch, or a socket issue. Pull the switch, straighten pins, reseat. - Binding keys: keycap interference (Cherry profile + north-facing LEDs can cause interference on some boards), or switch not seated flat, or too-thick films causing housing misclosure. Tuning steps if something’s off: 1) Re-seat switches and keycaps. 2) Address stabilizers (most common culprit for “bad sound”). 3) Adjust foam (add/remove). 4) Swap out any offending switches (you have spares). 5) Only then consider re-lubing a subset. If you want objective comparison, record a short typing sample before and after any change, from the same position, so you can hear differences. Anything else you’re unsure about—like north-facing LED interference with Cherry profile, or is your kit likely south-facing?
Good point. Assume south-facing, so no interference. I think that covers it. Thanks.

Then your plan is well-aligned: milky Gateron Yellows, light lube, optional films (test-fit first), TX screw-ins tuned carefully, minimal case foam, no PE. With gasket + PC + thick PBT, you should land in that clean, deep, controlled zone without a lot of iterative tweaks. If you later want to explore tactility, hot-swap makes it easy to try a few tactiles without committing to a full swap.
I have a BME280 sensor and an Arduino Uno. How do I wire it up and read temperature data?

If your BME280 breakout board supports I2C (most do), wiring is straightforward. 1) Identify pins on the BME280 breakout Common labels: VCC (or VIN), GND, SCL, SDA. Some boards also have CSB, SDO, and sometimes “3Vo”. 2) Wire for I2C to Arduino Uno - BME280 VCC/VIN -> Arduino 3.3V (preferred) or 5V only if the breakout has a regulator + level shifting - BME280 GND -> Arduino GND - BME280 SCL -> Arduino A5 (SCL) - BME280 SDA -> Arduino A4 (SDA) Notes: - The BME280 chip itself is 3.3V logic. Many breakouts include level shifting so they can tolerate 5V I2C. If you’re unsure, power it at 3.3V and keep I2C at 3.3V (but Uno I2C pins are 5V). In practice, most “Arduino-friendly” BME280 boards are safe on 5V, but check your board’s product page or silkscreen. 3) Install a library Two common options: - Adafruit BME280 Library (with Adafruit Unified Sensor) - SparkFun BME280 Library 4) Example code (Adafruit) In Arduino IDE: - Library Manager: install “Adafruit BME280 Library” and “Adafruit Unified Sensor” Then try: #include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; // I2C void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) { ; } if (!bme.begin(0x76)) { // common I2C addr: 0x76 or 0x77 Serial.println("Could not find a valid BME280 sensor, check wiring/address!"); while (1); } } void loop() { Serial.print("Temp C: "); Serial.println(bme.readTemperature()); delay(1000); } If it fails to detect, change 0x76 to 0x77. If you tell me what breakout board you have (any markings or a link) and whether you’re using 3.3V or 5V, I can confirm the safest wiring. Also: do you want humidity and pressure too, or temperature only?
I’d like to do temp, humidity, and pressure. The board says “GY-BME280” and it has pins: VCC, GND, SCL, SDA, CSB, SDO. I’m not sure about the address.

The GY-BME280 boards are common. They typically support both I2C and SPI. For I2C, you use VCC, GND, SCL, SDA, and you set/leave CSB and SDO appropriately. I2C mode selection: - CSB: For I2C mode, CSB should be tied HIGH (to VCC). Many breakouts already pull it up, so you can often leave it unconnected and it still works. But if you have trouble, explicitly connect CSB -> VCC. - SDO: In I2C mode, SDO selects the I2C address: - SDO = GND => address 0x76 - SDO = VCC => address 0x77 Some boards have a solder jumper; others just expose the pin. Safe wiring to Uno: - If your GY-BME280 board has a regulator/level shifting, VCC can go to 5V. But many GY boards are bare-bones and are 3.3V-only. If you have no documentation, the conservative approach is: - VCC -> Arduino 3.3V - GND -> GND - SCL -> A5 - SDA -> A4 - CSB -> 3.3V (optional but recommended if you see issues) - SDO -> GND (to force address 0x76) or 3.3V (for 0x77) Address detection: You can run an I2C scanner sketch to find it. Here’s a standard one: #include <Wire.h> void setup() { Wire.begin(); Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} Serial.println("I2C scanner"); } void loop() { byte error, address; int nDevices = 0; for (address = 1; address < 127; address++) { Wire.beginTransmission(address); error = Wire.endTransmission(); if (error == 0) { Serial.print("Found I2C device at 0x"); if (address < 16) Serial.print("0"); Serial.println(address, HEX); nDevices++; } } if (nDevices == 0) Serial.println("No I2C devices found"); else Serial.println("Done"); delay(3000); } Once you know whether it’s 0x76 or 0x77, use that in bme.begin(addr). Reading all three values with Adafruit library: Serial.print("Temp C: "); Serial.println(bme.readTemperature()); Serial.print("Humidity %: "); Serial.println(bme.readHumidity()); Serial.print("Pressure hPa: "); Serial.println(bme.readPressure() / 100.0F); One more detail: pressure can be calibrated to altitude if you set a sea-level pressure: bme.readAltitude(SEALEVELPRESSURE_HPA); Do you have any other I2C devices on the bus, or just the BME280 right now?
Just the BME280 for now. I ran the scanner and it didn’t find anything. Wiring is VCC to 3.3V, GND to GND, SDA to A4, SCL to A5. I left CSB and SDO floating.

If the scanner finds nothing, it’s usually one of: wrong power, wrong pins, missing pull-ups, sensor not in I2C mode, or a bad module. Let’s go step by step: 1) Confirm Uno I2C pins On Uno: - SDA is A4 - SCL is A5 You’ve got that right. 2) Ensure the sensor is actually powered With the board connected: - Do you see any power LED on the GY-BME280? Some have none. - If you have a multimeter: measure voltage between VCC and GND on the breakout; should be ~3.3V. 3) Force I2C mode and a known address Don’t leave CSB/SDO floating for debugging. Wire them explicitly: - CSB -> VCC (3.3V) to ensure I2C mode - SDO -> GND to choose address 0x76 (or VCC for 0x77) Then rerun scanner. 4) Pull-up resistors I2C requires pull-ups on SDA/SCL. Many breakout boards include pull-ups to 3.3V. Some don’t. The Uno has weak internal pull-ups, but they’re not always enough, and they pull to 5V if enabled. If your breakout lacks pull-ups, you can add: - 4.7k from SDA to 3.3V - 4.7k from SCL to 3.3V But try forcing CSB/SDO first. 5) Check for swapped SDA/SCL Sometimes the silkscreen is wrong (rare but happens). Try swapping SDA and SCL at the sensor end (sensor SDA->A5 and sensor SCL->A4) just as a test. 6) Try different Wire speed Some modules are flaky with long wires. Keep wires short. You can also set Wire clock to 100k: Wire.setClock(100000); 7) Hardware sanity Make sure you’re using the correct GND and 3.3V pins, not VIN. If you can answer: - Does your GY-BME280 board have a regulator chip (tiny 3-pin or 5-pin) and/or level shifter chips? Or is it just the sensor and a few resistors/caps? - How long are your jumper wires?

The board is tiny, has the metal sensor can, and I see one small chip that might be a regulator, plus a few resistors. Wires are like 20 cm jumpers. No LED. I tied CSB to 3.3V and SDO to GND like you said, still no devices found.

Given that, next I’d isolate whether it’s wiring/pins or the module. Quick checks: 1) Swap SDA/SCL as a test Even if labeled correctly, try: - Sensor SDA -> A5 - Sensor SCL -> A4 Keep VCC/GND same, CSB->3.3V, SDO->GND. Run scanner again. 2) Try powering from 5V (only briefly) if the board has a regulator Some GY boards are intended for 5V VCC because they have an onboard regulator (to 3.3V). If you feed them 3.3V, it still should work, but sometimes the regulator dropout or wiring causes undervoltage. If you’re reasonably confident there’s a regulator, try: - VCC -> 5V Keep I2C lines the same. Still keep CSB high and SDO low. If scanner finds it, great. If not, switch back. 3) Add pull-ups (if you have resistors) Even with a breakout, pull-ups can be missing or too weak. If you have 4.7k or 10k resistors: - SDA -> 3.3V via 4.7k - SCL -> 3.3V via 4.7k Then scan. 4) Check continuity / breadboard issues If you’re using a breadboard, sometimes the module pins aren’t making contact. Try direct female-to-male jumpers rather than breadboard rows, or re-seat everything. 5) Verify the Uno is actually running the scanner Open Serial Monitor at 9600. Confirm you see “I2C scanner” and periodic output. 6) Try a different I2C device or a known-good setup If you have any other I2C module (like an OLED), see if the scanner finds it. This checks whether the Uno I2C is working. 7) Alternative: Use SPI to confirm sensor works Since your breakout has CSB and SDO, it likely supports SPI. If I2C is giving trouble, you can test with SPI wiring and a library. SPI uses more wires but avoids I2C pull-up/address issues. SPI wiring (BME280 to Uno): - VCC -> 3.3V (or 5V if regulated) - GND -> GND - SCK -> D13 - SDI (MOSI) -> D11 - SDO (MISO) -> D12 - CSB -> D10 (chip select) But note: your board labels “SDA” and “SCL” might double as SDI/SCK in SPI mode. On some boards: SCL=SCK, SDA=SDI (MOSI). That’s confusing but common. Before we jump to SPI, can you try swapping SDA/SCL and tell me whether the scanner output changes? Also: are you connecting through a breadboard or directly with jumpers?

Through a breadboard, using male-male jumpers from Uno to breadboard then the module is pinned into the breadboard. I tried swapping SDA/SCL and still nothing. Serial monitor shows the scanner running and “No I2C devices found” every time. I can try SPI if you think that helps.

SPI is a good diagnostic: if SPI works, the sensor is alive and powered, and the issue is specific to I2C wiring/pull-ups/mode. First, identify SPI pin mapping on your GY-BME280 These boards often label pins for I2C (SCL/SDA) but those same pins are used for SPI: - SCL -> SCK (SPI clock) - SDA -> SDI (SPI MOSI, data into sensor) - SDO -> MISO (data out of sensor) - CSB -> CS (chip select) SPI wiring to Arduino Uno: - VCC -> 3.3V (start with 3.3V) - GND -> GND - SCL (SCK) -> D13 - SDA (SDI/MOSI) -> D11 - SDO (MISO) -> D12 - CSB (CS) -> D10 (or any digital pin, but D10 is typical) Library/code (Adafruit BME280 supports SPI) Install: - Adafruit BME280 - Adafruit Unified Sensor Example SPI code: #include <SPI.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #define BME_CS 10 Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS); // hardware SPI void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} if (!bme.begin()) { Serial.println("Could not find BME280 via SPI, check wiring!"); while (1); } } void loop() { Serial.print("Temp C: "); Serial.println(bme.readTemperature()); Serial.print("Hum %: "); Serial.println(bme.readHumidity()); Serial.print("Press hPa: "); Serial.println(bme.readPressure() / 100.0F); Serial.println("---"); delay(1000); } Important: - In SPI mode, CSB must be connected to a pin (chip select). Don’t tie CSB to VCC for SPI. - Keep wires short if possible. If SPI also fails, then we suspect power/ground contact on breadboard or a dead module. Before wiring: double-check the module pin order. Some breakouts have VCC and GND swapped from what you expect if viewed from the other side. Confirm you are reading the pin labels on the same side you’re plugging in. If you can, describe the pin order left-to-right as you’re viewing the text (e.g., VCC GND SCL SDA CSB SDO). Then I can confirm the mapping.

Pin order as printed on the board from left to right: VCC, GND, SCL, SDA, CSB, SDO. I wired SPI like you said: SCL to D13, SDA to D11, SDO to D12, CSB to D10, VCC to 3.3V, GND to GND. The code still says it can’t find BME280 via SPI.

If both I2C and SPI fail, focus on fundamentals: power, ground, and physical contact. 1) Breadboard contact / pin fit Some modules have thin pins or slightly oxidized headers that don’t reliably contact a breadboard. - Try pushing the module firmly and evenly into the breadboard. - Try a different breadboard row. - If you have female-to-male jumpers, bypass the breadboard: connect jumpers directly to the module header pins (if pins are installed) and to the Uno. 2) Confirm power at the module If you have a multimeter: - Measure VCC to GND at the module pins: should read ~3.3V. - If it’s significantly lower (e.g., 2.7V), the module might not be getting enough power due to a bad connection. 3) Try VCC at 5V (if regulator exists) Many GY modules include an AMS1117-3.3 or similar regulator. If that’s the “small chip” you saw, it might actually be a 3.3V regulator. But note: AMS1117 is usually bigger (SOT-223 package). If yours is tiny, it might be an LDO in SOT-23-5. Try: - VCC -> 5V Keep the rest the same (SPI wiring). If it suddenly works, then your 3.3V feed wasn’t sufficient or the module expects 5V input. 4) Check for shorts Inspect the solder joints and the board for solder bridges, especially around the header pins. 5) Confirm you’re using the correct bme.begin() for SPI In Adafruit library: - For SPI constructor Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS); then bme.begin() with no address is correct. But sometimes you must specify SPI frequency or use the explicit constructor: Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS, 11, 12, 13); // software SPI Try software SPI to rule out a hardware SPI pin mismatch: #define BME_CS 10 #define BME_MOSI 11 #define BME_MISO 12 #define BME_SCK 13 Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS, BME_MOSI, BME_MISO, BME_SCK); Then in setup: if (!bme.begin()) { ... } 6) Sensor type confusion: BMP280 vs BME280 Some boards are mislabeled. If it’s actually BMP280 (no humidity), the BME280 library might fail detection depending on chip ID. The Adafruit library usually checks chip ID; BMP280 returns different ID. Try using Adafruit BMP280 library as a test. Or in BME280 library, there’s sometimes a “force” mode; but easiest is to try BMP280 library quickly. 7) Read chip ID directly (advanced but definitive) Over SPI, you can read register 0xD0 (chip ID). For BME280, it’s 0x60. For BMP280, it’s 0x58. If you can read that, the wiring is correct. Would you like to try the “read chip ID” sketch over SPI? It’s a short program and it will tell us whether the sensor responds at all.
Yes, let’s do chip ID over SPI. I have no multimeter right now, but I can re-seat and try direct jumpers too.

Here’s a minimal SPI chip-ID reader. It uses hardware SPI on Uno (D13 SCK, D12 MISO, D11 MOSI). We’ll use D10 as CS. Wiring reminder: - VCC -> 3.3V (or 5V if you want to try after) - GND -> GND - SCL(SCK) -> D13 - SDA(MOSI/SDI) -> D11 - SDO(MISO) -> D12 - CSB(CS) -> D10 Code: #include <SPI.h> const int CS_PIN = 10; void spiWrite(byte reg, byte val) { digitalWrite(CS_PIN, LOW); SPI.transfer(reg & 0x7F); // write: bit7=0 SPI.transfer(val); digitalWrite(CS_PIN, HIGH); } byte spiRead(byte reg) { digitalWrite(CS_PIN, LOW); SPI.transfer(reg | 0x80); // read: bit7=1 byte val = SPI.transfer(0x00); digitalWrite(CS_PIN, HIGH); return val; } void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} pinMode(CS_PIN, OUTPUT); digitalWrite(CS_PIN, HIGH); SPI.begin(); SPI.beginTransaction(SPISettings(1000000, MSBFIRST, SPI_MODE0)); delay(10); byte id = spiRead(0xD0); Serial.print("Chip ID (0xD0): 0x"); if (id < 16) Serial.print("0"); Serial.println(id, HEX); // Optional: read reset register or status byte status = spiRead(0xF3); Serial.print("Status (0xF3): 0x"); if (status < 16) Serial.print("0"); Serial.println(status, HEX); } void loop() { } Expected: - BME280: 0x60 - BMP280: 0x58 - If you get 0xFF or 0x00 consistently, that often indicates no communication (MISO floating or CS wrong). Try this with the module connected directly via jumpers if you can (bypassing breadboard). Then tell me the printed chip ID.
I bypassed the breadboard with female-to-male jumpers directly to the module pins. Now the chip ID reads 0x60! Status is 0x00. So it’s alive. Why didn’t the library detect it earlier?

Great—0x60 confirms it’s a real BME280 and SPI wiring is basically correct. The earlier failures were likely due to unreliable breadboard contact or a CS/power connection issue through the breadboard. Why the library didn’t detect it earlier: - Intermittent connections on breadboard: a single flaky MISO, MOSI, SCK, CS, VCC, or GND line will make the ID read fail, and the library will report “not found”. - CSB pin not making good contact is especially common; if CS is floating, the sensor won’t respond reliably. - Power rail splits on some breadboards: sometimes the top/bottom rails are not connected, or a rail is broken in the middle. That can lead to “it’s powered sometimes” behavior. Now that you have a working direct connection, you should be able to use the Adafruit BME280 library over SPI. Try this known-good SPI example (hardware SPI): #include <SPI.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #define BME_CS 10 Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS); void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} if (!bme.begin()) { Serial.println("BME280 not found"); while (1); } Serial.println("BME280 OK"); } void loop() { Serial.print("T(C): "); Serial.println(bme.readTemperature(), 2); Serial.print("RH(%): "); Serial.println(bme.readHumidity(), 2); Serial.print("P(hPa): "); Serial.println(bme.readPressure() / 100.0F, 2); Serial.println(); delay(1000); } If you want I2C (fewer wires), we can go back and make I2C reliable too, but SPI is perfectly fine for a weather station. Next steps for a weather station: - Add a display (I2C OLED 0.96" SSD1306 is common) - Log to SD card - Add a real-time clock (DS3231) for timestamps - Add wireless (ESP8266/ESP32 or an NRF24L01) What direction do you want: display first, data logging, or uploading data to a PC/cloud?
Display first. I have a 0.96" OLED that says SSD1306 I2C. How do I wire that alongside the BME280 if I’m using SPI for the sensor?

Perfect combination: OLED on I2C, BME280 on SPI. They won’t conflict. OLED I2C wiring to Uno: - VCC -> 5V (most SSD1306 modules accept 3.3–5V; check yours) - GND -> GND - SCL -> A5 - SDA -> A4 Keep BME280 SPI wiring as you have it: - BME VCC -> 3.3V - BME GND -> GND - BME SCK -> D13 - BME MOSI -> D11 - BME MISO -> D12 - BME CS -> D10 They can share GND (must share). Libraries: - Adafruit SSD1306 - Adafruit GFX (And keep Adafruit BME280) OLED address: Most are 0x3C, sometimes 0x3D. You can use your I2C scanner now (with OLED connected) to confirm. Example combined sketch (simple text): #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #include <Adafruit_GFX.h> #include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h> #define SCREEN_WIDTH 128 #define SCREEN_HEIGHT 64 #define OLED_RESET -1 Adafruit_SSD1306 display(SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT, &Wire, OLED_RESET); #define BME_CS 10 Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS); void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); // OLED init if (!display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C)) { Serial.println("SSD1306 allocation failed"); while (1); } display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); // BME init if (!bme.begin()) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.println("BME280 not found"); display.display(); while (1); } display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.println("Weather station"); display.display(); delay(1000); } void loop() { float t = bme.readTemperature(); float h = bme.readHumidity(); float p = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("T: "); display.print(t, 1); display.println(" C"); display.print("RH: "); display.print(h, 1); display.println(" %"); display.print("P: "); display.print(p, 1); display.println(" hPa"); display.display(); delay(1000); } If your OLED is 0x3D, change 0x3C to 0x3D. If you see nothing on the OLED: - Confirm VCC/GND orientation - Run I2C scanner with only OLED connected - Some OLED modules label SCL/SDA swapped (rare); you can test swap. Do you know whether your OLED module is 4-pin (VCC GND SCL SDA) or does it have extra pins?
It’s 4-pin. I wired it and the screen lights up but stays blank. The serial monitor shows nothing wrong. I2C scanner finds a device at 0x3C.

If the scanner sees 0x3C, wiring is correct. Blank screen usually comes down to initialization parameters or display contrast/memory addressing issues, but with Adafruit SSD1306 it usually “just works” if the correct geometry is used. Common causes and fixes: 1) Wrong screen height Some 0.96" OLEDs are 128x64, others are 128x32. If you initialize 128x64 but it’s actually 128x32, it can appear blank or glitchy. Try SCREEN_HEIGHT 32: #define SCREEN_WIDTH 128 #define SCREEN_HEIGHT 32 2) Missing display.display() You have it in loop, so that’s fine. 3) Power type Adafruit library uses SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC for internal charge pump (most modules). If your module expects external VCC without charge pump (rare), you’d use SSD1306_EXTERNALVCC. But most 0.96" I2C modules are SWITCHCAPVCC. Try: display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C); If blank, try: display.begin(SSD1306_EXTERNALVCC, 0x3C); 4) Reset pin You set OLED_RESET = -1 (no reset pin). That’s correct for many 4-pin modules. But some boards still have a reset line tied in a weird way. You can try manually toggling by power cycling. 5) Contrast You can try setting contrast explicitly after begin: display.ssd1306_command(SSD1306_SETCONTRAST); display.ssd1306_command(0xFF); 6) Quick known-good test Before mixing with BME280, run the Adafruit SSD1306 library example: File -> Examples -> Adafruit SSD1306 -> ssd1306_128x64_i2c (or 128x32). Make sure that works first. Given your symptoms, I’d first bet on 128x32 vs 128x64 mismatch. Many 0.96" are 128x64, but a lot of the skinny ones are 128x32. Can you describe the screen: is it “tall-ish” (64px height) or more like a narrow strip (32px)? Also, when you say it “lights up,” does it show a faint line or just a uniformly dark background?
It’s more like a narrow strip, so probably 128x32. Background is dark but no text. I’ll change to 32.

Yes—set SCREEN_HEIGHT to 32 and use the 128x32 example if possible. Minimal OLED-only test (128x32): #include <Wire.h> #include <Adafruit_GFX.h> #include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h> #define SCREEN_WIDTH 128 #define SCREEN_HEIGHT 32 #define OLED_RESET -1 Adafruit_SSD1306 display(SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT, &Wire, OLED_RESET); void setup() { if (!display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, 0x3C)) { while (1); } display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0,0); display.println("OLED OK"); display.println("Addr: 0x3C"); display.display(); } void loop() {} Once that displays, merge back with the BME280 SPI code. One more tip: because the OLED uses I2C and BME uses SPI, they can coexist without special handling. Just don’t reuse pins. Let me know if “OLED OK” shows up after changing to 32.
That worked. Now the combined code shows readings. Great. Next I want it to show pressure trend, like rising/falling arrow. How can I do that?

A simple pressure trend indicator compares current pressure to an earlier pressure (e.g., 10 minutes ago) and applies a threshold to avoid noise. Approach: - Sample pressure once per second (or every few seconds). - Keep a rolling history buffer (ring buffer) of pressure values at a longer interval (e.g., store one value per minute). - Compare the latest value to the value N minutes ago. - If difference > +Δ => rising - If difference < -Δ => falling - Else steady Typical thresholds: - 0.5 hPa over 10 minutes is noticeable but not too noisy. You can tune based on your environment. Example implementation: - Take a pressure reading every second. - Every 60 seconds, store average pressure into an array of length 15 (15 minutes). - Trend compares current minute average to value 10 minutes ago. Code sketch (integrate into your loop): const int HISTORY_LEN = 15; float pHist[HISTORY_LEN]; int histIndex = 0; bool histFilled = false; unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; unsigned long lastMinuteMs = 0; float pAccum = 0; int pCount = 0; void updatePressureHistory(float p) { unsigned long now = millis(); // accumulate per-second samples if (now - lastSampleMs >= 1000) { lastSampleMs = now; pAccum += p; pCount++; } // every 60 seconds, store minute average if (now - lastMinuteMs >= 60000) { lastMinuteMs = now; if (pCount > 0) { float pAvg = pAccum / pCount; pHist[histIndex] = pAvg; histIndex = (histIndex + 1) % HISTORY_LEN; if (histIndex == 0) histFilled = true; } pAccum = 0; pCount = 0; } } // returns -1 falling, 0 steady, +1 rising int pressureTrend() { int minutesBack = 10; if (!histFilled && histIndex < minutesBack) return 0; // not enough data int currentIndex = (histIndex - 1 + HISTORY_LEN) % HISTORY_LEN; int pastIndex = (histIndex - 1 - minutesBack + HISTORY_LEN) % HISTORY_LEN; float pNow = pHist[currentIndex]; float pPast = pHist[pastIndex]; float diff = pNow - pPast; float threshold = 0.5; // hPa over 10 minutes if (diff > threshold) return +1; if (diff < -threshold) return -1; return 0; } Then in loop: - Read pressure p = bme.readPressure()/100.0F; - Call updatePressureHistory(p); - Determine trend = pressureTrend(); - Display arrow: "^", "v", "-" or custom glyph. On SSD1306, easiest is text: - Rising: "P: 1012.3 ^" - Falling: "P: 1012.3 v" - Steady: "P: 1012.3 -" If you want a nicer arrow, you can draw triangles with display.fillTriangle. Do you want the trend based on 10 minutes, 30 minutes, or something else? And do you want it to update every second or only every minute?
10 minutes is fine. I also want to compute dew point and show it. Can you give me the formula and code?

Dew point can be estimated from temperature and relative humidity. A common accurate-enough formula for typical weather station ranges is the Magnus formula. Given: - T in °C - RH in % (0–100) Constants (for water over liquid): a = 17.62 b = 243.12 °C Compute: gamma = (a*T)/(b+T) + ln(RH/100) dewPoint = (b*gamma)/(a - gamma) Arduino code: #include <math.h> float dewPointC(float tempC, float humidity) { // humidity in percent const float a = 17.62; const float b = 243.12; float rh = humidity / 100.0; if (rh <= 0.0) rh = 0.0001; // avoid log(0) float gamma = (a * tempC) / (b + tempC) + log(rh); float dp = (b * gamma) / (a - gamma); return dp; } Then: float t = bme.readTemperature(); float h = bme.readHumidity(); float dp = dewPointC(t, h); Display: display.print("DP: "); display.print(dp, 1); display.println(" C"); Notes: - If humidity reading is 0 or invalid, dew point will be nonsense; clamp RH. - Dew point is sensitive to RH accuracy; expect some variation. Do you also want “heat index” or “absolute humidity”? Those are other common computed metrics.
Maybe later. Right now, the readings jump around a bit. Temperature fluctuates by 0.2-0.3C. Is there smoothing I can do without making it lag too much?

Yes—use a lightweight filter. Two common options: 1) Moving average (simple, some lag) Average the last N samples (e.g., N=5 if sampling every second gives ~5s smoothing). 2) Exponential moving average (EMA) / low-pass filter (less memory, tunable) filtered = filtered + alpha*(raw - filtered) Alpha between 0 and 1. Smaller alpha = smoother but more lag. For 1 Hz sampling, alpha ~0.2 is mild smoothing; 0.1 is smoother. EMA example: float tFilt = NAN, hFilt = NAN, pFilt = NAN; float ema(float prev, float x, float alpha) { if (isnan(prev)) return x; return prev + alpha * (x - prev); } In loop (each second): float t = bme.readTemperature(); float h = bme.readHumidity(); float p = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; float alphaT = 0.2; float alphaH = 0.2; float alphaP = 0.1; tFilt = ema(tFilt, t, alphaT); hFilt = ema(hFilt, h, alphaH); pFilt = ema(pFilt, p, alphaP); Then display filtered values. If you also compute dew point, compute it from filtered T and RH for stability: dp = dewPointC(tFilt, hFilt); Tip: Keep your sampling interval steady (e.g., once per second using millis timing) so the filter behavior is consistent. Do you want smoothing on all values or only temperature/humidity?
Temperature and humidity mainly. Also, I want to add a DS3231 RTC for time stamps later. Will that conflict with the OLED I2C?

DS3231 is also I2C, so it can share the same SDA/SCL lines as the OLED. That’s the whole point of I2C: multiple devices on the same two wires, each with its own address. Typical addresses: - SSD1306 OLED: 0x3C (or 0x3D) - DS3231: 0x68 Wiring DS3231 to Uno: - VCC -> 5V (most DS3231 modules accept 5V; the chip itself is 3.3V but modules usually have regulation/level shifting) - GND -> GND - SDA -> A4 - SCL -> A5 Potential issues: - Too many pull-ups: OLED and DS3231 boards often each include pull-up resistors. Having both can make pull-ups too strong (low resistance), increasing current but usually still works at 5V and short wires. If you get I2C instability, you can remove/disable pull-ups on one module (often by cutting a jumper) or just keep wires short. - Voltage levels: If one board pulls up to 3.3V and another to 5V, that can be a problem. Most OLED modules pull up to VCC. DS3231 modules usually pull up to VCC. If both are at 5V, you’re consistent. If you power OLED at 3.3V and DS3231 at 5V, the pull-ups fight. So: power both at 5V if they’re 5V-tolerant modules, or both at 3.3V if you’re sure they work at 3.3V. Since you’re on an Uno and already powering OLED at 5V, you can also power DS3231 at 5V and keep the I2C bus at 5V. That’s fine for typical modules. When you add DS3231, run the I2C scanner again; you should see 0x3C and 0x68. When you’re ready, I can provide code to set the time and display timestamps.
Great. Another question: I’m thinking of powering this from a USB power bank. Any issues with power draw? OLED + Uno + BME280.

Power draw is usually modest: - Arduino Uno: often ~40–70 mA depending on onboard regulator/USB-serial chip and what’s connected. - SSD1306 0.96" OLED: ~10–25 mA depending on brightness and how many pixels are lit. - BME280: very low (typically <1 mA average in normal mode; microamps in sleep).
