So total might be ~60–100 mA typical, which is fine for a power bank. Considerations: 1) Power bank auto-shutoff Some power banks turn off if current draw is too low (e.g., under ~50–100 mA). With an Uno + OLED, you’re likely above that threshold, but if you later optimize power (sleep modes, turning off OLED), you might drop below and the bank could shut off. 2) Noise and stability Power banks are usually stable 5V, but some have switching noise. Usually not an issue for these sensors, but if you see flicker or I2C glitches, add a decoupling capacitor (e.g., 100 µF across 5V and GND near the Uno). 3) Wiring Keep ground common and avoid long thin wires if you can. 4) If you later switch to a lower-power board A Pro Mini 3.3V or an ESP32 can reduce power or add Wi-Fi, but the Uno is fine for learning. Do you plan to keep it as a portable station, or eventually mount it somewhere and run it continuously?
Portable for now. I want to make the code cleaner. Right now everything is in loop. Can you suggest a structure: separate sensor reading, filtering, display update, etc.?

A clean beginner-friendly structure is to: - Use a fixed “task” schedule using millis() - Keep state in a struct - Separate functions: readSensors(), updateFilters(), computeDerived(), updateDisplay() Example outline: 1) Define a struct to hold raw and filtered values: struct WeatherData { float tRaw, hRaw, pRaw; float t, h, p;     // filtered float dewPoint; int trend;         // -1,0,1 }; WeatherData wd; 2) Timing variables: const unsigned long SENSOR_PERIOD_MS = 1000; const unsigned long DISPLAY_PERIOD_MS = 500; // OLED refresh unsigned long lastSensorMs = 0; unsigned long lastDisplayMs = 0; 3) Functions: float ema(float prev, float x, float alpha) { ... } void readSensors(WeatherData &w) { w.tRaw = bme.readTemperature(); w.hRaw = bme.readHumidity(); w.pRaw = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; } void updateFilters(WeatherData &w) { w.t = ema(w.t, w.tRaw, 0.2); w.h = ema(w.h, w.hRaw, 0.2); w.p = ema(w.p, w.pRaw, 0.1); } float dewPointC(float t, float rh) { ... } void computeDerived(WeatherData &w) { w.dewPoint = dewPointC(w.t, w.h); // trend updated elsewhere if using minute history } void updateDisplay(const WeatherData &w) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("T: "); display.print(w.t, 1); display.println(" C"); display.print("RH: "); display.print(w.h, 1); display.println(" %"); display.print("P: "); display.print(w.p, 1); display.print(" "); if (w.trend > 0) display.println("^"); else if (w.trend < 0) display.println("v"); else display.println("-"); display.print("DP: "); display.print(w.dewPoint, 1); display.println(" C"); display.display(); } 4) In loop: void loop() { unsigned long now = millis(); if (now - lastSensorMs >= SENSOR_PERIOD_MS) { lastSensorMs = now; readSensors(wd); updateFilters(wd); computeDerived(wd); // update trend history here too } if (now - lastDisplayMs >= DISPLAY_PERIOD_MS) { lastDisplayMs = now; updateDisplay(wd); } } This keeps the display updating smoothly even if you later add slower tasks (logging, RTC). If you want, I can help you integrate the 10-minute pressure trend buffer into this structure cleanly (e.g., a PressureTrend class with update() and getTrend()).
Yes, integrate it cleanly. Also, I want to avoid floats where possible, but I know BME280 gives floats. Is it worth converting to fixed point?

For an Uno, floats are slower but still fine at 1 Hz updates. Converting to fixed-point is optional; it can make code more complex. A good compromise is: - Keep floats internally for sensor readings and derived calculations. - Convert to integers for display/logging (e.g., tenths of degrees, tenths of hPa) if you want stable formatting and smaller logs. Clean pressure trend integration: Make a small class that stores minute averages and computes trend. Example: class PressureTrend { public: void begin() { for (int i=0;i<HIST_LEN;i++) hist[i]=NAN; } // call once per sensor sample (e.g., each second) void update(float pHpa) { unsigned long now = millis(); // accumulate if (now - lastSampleMs >= 1000) { lastSampleMs = now; accum += pHpa; count++; } // every minute store avg if (now - lastMinuteMs >= 60000) { lastMinuteMs = now; if (count > 0) { float avg = accum / count; hist[idx] = avg; idx = (idx + 1) % HIST_LEN; if (idx == 0) filled = true; } accum = 0; count = 0; } } int trend10min(float thresholdHpa = 0.5) const { const int minutesBack = 10; if (!filled && idx <= minutesBack) return 0; int current = (idx - 1 + HIST_LEN) % HIST_LEN; int past = (idx - 1 - minutesBack + HIST_LEN) % HIST_LEN; float pNow = hist[current]; float pPast = hist[past]; if (isnan(pNow) || isnan(pPast)) return 0; float diff = pNow - pPast; if (diff > thresholdHpa) return +1; if (diff < -thresholdHpa) return -1; return 0; } private: static const int HIST_LEN = 15; float hist[HIST_LEN]; int idx = 0; bool filled = false; unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; unsigned long lastMinuteMs = 0; float accum = 0; int count = 0; }; Then in your main code: PressureTrend pTrend; In setup: pTrend.begin(); In sensor update block: pTrend.update(wd.p); // or use raw pRaw, but filtered is okay wd.trend = pTrend.trend10min(0.5); About fixed point: If you want, you can store “scaled integers” after filtering: - t10 = (int)round(wd.t * 10) // tenths of °C - h10 = (int)round(wd.h * 10) // tenths of % - p10 = (int)round(wd.p * 10) // tenths of hPa Then display as p10/10 with remainder. But because the filter itself uses floats, you won’t eliminate floats unless you re-implement EMA in fixed point too. That’s doable, but I wouldn’t recommend it for a first project. Do you want the trend threshold configurable via a button/menu later, or hardcoded is fine?
Hardcoded is fine. Now, I noticed the pressure reading changes when I touch the sensor board. Is that normal?

Yes, that can happen for a few reasons: 1) Temperature coupling When you touch the board, you warm it slightly. The BME280 measures temperature internally, and temperature affects pressure measurement compensation. So pressure can shift a bit as temperature changes. 2) Mechanical stress / airflow Touching can create small pressure changes (air movement) around the sensor port, especially if your finger partially blocks airflow or creates a micro “pump” effect. 3) Self-heating / board layout If the sensor is near warm components (like regulators), readings can drift. Touching changes heat flow. Mitigations: - Mount the sensor so air can circulate around it; don’t enclose it tightly. - Avoid placing it right above the Uno’s voltage regulator or other heat sources. - Use a short cable and position the sensor away from the main board. - If you make an enclosure, include ventilation holes and keep the sensor near a vent. For a weather station, it’s common to put the sensor in a ventilated “radiation shield” if measuring ambient conditions accurately. Are you aiming for “room monitoring” accuracy or more outdoor-like ambient accuracy (even if you’re keeping it portable)?
Room monitoring. I also want to add a small buzzer that beeps if humidity goes above a threshold. Any simple way?

Yes. Use a piezo buzzer on a digital pin and check the filtered humidity. Hardware: - Piezo buzzer module: VCC, GND, SIG (easy) - Or raw piezo element: connect one lead to a pin and the other to GND (often fine), but better with a resistor (~100–220Ω) in series. Simplest with Arduino tone(): - Connect buzzer positive to D6 (for example), negative to GND. Code: const int BUZZ_PIN = 6; const float HUM_ALARM = 70.0; // % In setup: pinMode(BUZZ_PIN, OUTPUT); In loop after computing wd.h: static bool alarmOn = false; if (wd.h > HUM_ALARM) { if (!alarmOn) { tone(BUZZ_PIN, 2000, 200); // 2kHz beep 200ms alarmOn = true; } } else { alarmOn = false; noTone(BUZZ_PIN); } This does a single beep when crossing above threshold. If you want periodic beeps while above threshold, use a timer: unsigned long lastBeep = 0; if (wd.h > HUM_ALARM) { if (millis() - lastBeep > 5000) { tone(BUZZ_PIN, 2000, 200); lastBeep = millis(); } } Do you have a buzzer module or just a bare piezo disc?
Bare piezo disc. Also I want to add buttons to switch between screens (main, min/max, trend graph). How should I wire buttons?

For buttons on Uno, easiest is to use the internal pull-up resistors. Wiring (per button): - One side of button -> GND - Other side -> a digital input pin (e.g., D2, D3, D4) In code: pinMode(pin, INPUT_PULLUP); Then pressed = LOW, released = HIGH. This avoids external resistors. Debouncing: Buttons bounce, so implement debounce either by: - simple delay (not ideal) - state + time method Example debounce for one button: const int BTN_PIN = 2; bool btnState = HIGH; bool lastReading = HIGH; unsigned long lastDebounceMs = 0; const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 30; void setup() { pinMode(BTN_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); } bool buttonPressedEvent() { bool reading = digitalRead(BTN_PIN); if (reading != lastReading) { lastDebounceMs = millis(); lastReading = reading; } if ((millis() - lastDebounceMs) > DEBOUNCE_MS) { if (reading != btnState) { btnState = reading; if (btnState == LOW) return true; // pressed event } } return false; } For multiple buttons, make a small struct or class per button. Screen switching: - Keep an enum Screen { MAIN, MINMAX, GRAPH }; - On press, increment screen and wrap. Min/max: - Track min/max for temperature/humidity/pressure since startup or per day (if you add RTC). Trend graph: - You can draw a tiny line chart of the last N minutes pressure values. Since you already have minute history, reuse that. Do you want 1 button to cycle screens, or 2 buttons (next/prev)?
One button to cycle is fine. For the trend graph, can you show how to draw a simple graph on 128x32?

Yes. On 128x32, you can allocate maybe the bottom 16 pixels for a graph and top lines for text, or make a full-screen graph mode. Simple full-screen pressure graph: - Use your minute history array (e.g., 60 minutes would be nice, but you currently have 15). For 128 pixels wide, you can map one minute per ~8 pixels if 15 minutes. - Find min and max pressure in the buffer to scale vertically. - Draw a line between successive points. Example function assuming you have an array hist[] of length N with newest at some index. For simplicity, we’ll build a local ordered array oldest->newest. Pseudo-code: void drawPressureGraph(const float *hist, int N, int newestIndex) { // Build ordered data float data[N]; for (int i=0; i<N; i++) { int idx = (newestIndex - (N-1-i) + N) % N; // oldest to newest data[i] = hist[idx]; } // Find min/max float pMin = data[0], pMax = data[0]; for (int i=1; i<N; i++) { if (data[i] < pMin) pMin = data[i]; if (data[i] > pMax) pMax = data[i]; } if (pMax - pMin < 0.1) { pMax = pMin + 0.1; } // avoid div0 display.clearDisplay(); // Draw axes (optional) display.drawRect(0, 0, 128, 32, SSD1306_WHITE); // Plot area inside border int x0 = 1, y0 = 1, w = 126, h = 30; for (int i=0; i<N; i++) { int x = x0 + (long)i * (w - 1) / (N - 1); float norm = (data[i] - pMin) / (pMax - pMin); // 0..1 int y = y0 + (h - 1) - (int)round(norm * (h - 1)); // Draw point display.drawPixel(x, y, SSD1306_WHITE); // Draw line from previous if (i > 0) { int xPrev = x0 + (long)(i-1) * (w - 1) / (N - 1); float normPrev = (data[i-1] - pMin) / (pMax - pMin); int yPrev = y0 + (h - 1) - (int)round(normPrev * (h - 1)); display.drawLine(xPrev, yPrev, x, y, SSD1306_WHITE); } } // Label min/max (tiny text) display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(2, 2); display.print(pMax, 1); display.setCursor(2, 22); display.print(pMin, 1); display.display(); } To integrate with your PressureTrend class: - Expose a method to copy out the history or provide accessors: - getHist(i) to get i-th sample - getNewestIndex() Or store history in your main code instead. If you want a longer graph (e.g., last 60 minutes), increase HIST_LEN to 60. That’s 60 floats = 240 bytes, fine on Uno. Do you want the graph mode to show a time span label (e.g., “60 min”)?
Yes, 60 minutes would be better. Let’s change HIST_LEN to 60. Will memory be okay with OLED buffer too?

On Uno (2 KB SRAM), you need to be mindful: - SSD1306 128x32 buffer: 128*32/8 = 512 bytes. - Pressure history 60 floats: 60*4 = 240 bytes. - BME280 library + other globals: some overhead. You should still be okay, but avoid large additional buffers. So yes, 60 should fit. Update PressureTrend: - HIST_LEN = 60 - Trend comparison: still 10 minutes back; that’s fine. - For graph, you’ll have 60 points; mapping 60 points to 126 pixels gives ~2 pixels per point, looks decent. One improvement: store pressure as int16 in tenths of hPa to save memory: - 60 * 2 = 120 bytes instead of 240. But floats are still okay at 60. If you want to keep it simple, stick with floats for now. Graph mode label: display.setCursor(80, 2); display.print("60m"); Also, scaling: if pMin and pMax are very close, graph will look flat; you can enforce a minimum range like 1.0 hPa to make small changes visible: float range = pMax - pMin; if (range < 1.0) { float mid=(pMax+pMin)/2; pMin=mid-0.5; pMax=mid+0.5; } Do you want the graph to auto-scale like that, or represent absolute pressure range (which might look flat most of the time)?
Auto-scale. Also, I want min/max screen that resets daily when I add the RTC. For now, reset on button long press. How do I detect long press?

With INPUT_PULLUP button, you can detect press duration by recording the millis() when it goes LOW and checking how long it stays LOW. Concept: - On press down: store pressStartMs - While held: if now - pressStartMs > LONG_MS and not yet handled => long press event - On release: if not long handled and held time < LONG_MS => short press event Example for one button: const int BTN_PIN = 2; const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 30; const unsigned long LONG_MS = 800; bool stableState = HIGH; bool lastReading = HIGH; unsigned long lastDebounceMs = 0; unsigned long pressStartMs = 0; bool longFired = false; enum BtnEvent { NONE, SHORT_PRESS, LONG_PRESS }; BtnEvent updateButton() { BtnEvent ev = NONE; bool reading = digitalRead(BTN_PIN); if (reading != lastReading) { lastDebounceMs = millis(); lastReading = reading; } if (millis() - lastDebounceMs > DEBOUNCE_MS) { if (reading != stableState) { stableState = reading; if (stableState == LOW) { // pressed pressStartMs = millis(); longFired = false; } else { // released if (!longFired) ev = SHORT_PRESS; } } else { // stable, check long if (stableState == LOW && !longFired) { if (millis() - pressStartMs > LONG_MS) { longFired = true; ev = LONG_PRESS; } } } } return ev; } Usage: BtnEvent ev = updateButton(); if (ev == SHORT_PRESS) cycleScreen(); if (ev == LONG_PRESS) resetMinMax(); Min/max tracking: Keep variables like: float tMin, tMax, hMin, hMax, pMin, pMax; Initialize them to NAN and update each sensor cycle: if (isnan(tMin) || wd.t < tMin) tMin = wd.t; ... Reset sets them to current values or NAN. When you add RTC later: - At midnight change (date changes), call resetMinMax automatically. Do you want min/max per metric (T/RH/P) or just temperature and humidity?
All three. Now can you help me make the display layout nicer? On 128x32 it’s cramped. Maybe show two screens: one for T/RH/DP, another for P and trend arrow and graph.

Yes—split into modes: Screen 1 (Comfort): Line1: T: xx.xC  RH: yy% Line2: DP: xx.xC  (optional: maybe show “Humid” etc.) Screen 2 (Pressure): Line1: P: xxxx.x hPa  ^/v/- Line2: small mini-graph across bottom (sparkline) On 128x32, you have 4 text rows at size 1 (8 px tall each). You can do: - Top 16 px for text (2 rows) - Bottom 16 px for sparkline graph Sparkline approach: - Plot last 60 minutes into height 14 px (leaving 1 px border) - Draw without labels to save space Example pressure screen drawing: void drawPressureScreen(float p, int trend, const float *data, int N, int newestIdx) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("P:"); display.print(p, 1); display.print("hPa "); if (trend > 0) display.print("^"); else if (trend < 0) display.print("v"); else display.print("-"); display.setCursor(80,0); display.print("60m"); // Sparkline area: y from 16 to 31 int x0 = 0, y0 = 16, w = 128, h = 16; // Build ordered min/max float pMin = 1e9, pMax = -1e9; for (int i=0;i<N;i++) { int idx = (newestIdx - (N-1-i) + N) % N; float v = data[idx]; if (isnan(v)) continue; if (v < pMin) pMin = v; if (v > pMax) pMax = v; } if (pMax < pMin) { pMin = p - 0.5; pMax = p + 0.5; } float range = pMax - pMin; if (range < 1.0) { float mid = (pMax + pMin) * 0.5; pMin = mid - 0.5; pMax = mid + 0.5; range = 1.0; } int prevX = -1, prevY = -1; for (int i=0;i<N;i++) { int idx = (newestIdx - (N-1-i) + N) % N; float v = data[idx]; if (isnan(v)) continue; int x = x0 + (long)i * (w - 1) / (N - 1); float norm = (v - pMin) / range; int y = y0 + (h - 1) - (int)round(norm * (h - 1)); if (prevX >= 0) display.drawLine(prevX, prevY, x, y, SSD1306_WHITE); prevX = x; prevY = y; } display.display(); } Comfort screen: void drawComfortScreen(float t, float rh, float dp) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("T:"); display.print(t,1); display.print("C "); display.print("RH:"); display.print(rh,0); display.print("%"); display.setCursor(0,16); display.print("DP:"); display.print(dp,1); display.print("C"); display.display(); } You can also add a simple comfort label: - If dew point > 18C => “muggy” - < 10C => “dry” But optional. To do this, your PressureTrend class should expose: - a method to get pointer to hist array and current idx (newest index is idx-1) or provide a function that returns value by age. If you want, I can rewrite PressureTrend to include: const float* buffer() const; int size() const; int newestIndex() const;

Yes, rewrite PressureTrend with those accessors. Also please show a full sketch that compiles with everything: BME280 SPI, OLED I2C 128x32, one button, buzzer, EMA smoothing, dew point, pressure trend and sparkline, min/max with long press reset.

Here’s a full integrated sketch. You may need to adjust pin numbers to match your wiring (CS pin for BME280, button pin, buzzer pin). This assumes: - BME280 SPI CS on D10 - OLED I2C at 0x3C, 128x32 - Button on D2 to GND (INPUT_PULLUP) - Piezo on D6 to GND Libraries needed: - Adafruit_BME280 - Adafruit_SSD1306 - Adafruit_GFX - Adafruit Unified Sensor Full sketch: #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <math.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #include <Adafruit_GFX.h> #include <Adafruit_SSD1306.h> // ---------- Pins ---------- #define BME_CS    10 #define BTN_PIN   2 #define BUZZ_PIN  6 // ---------- OLED ---------- #define SCREEN_WIDTH 128 #define SCREEN_HEIGHT 32 #define OLED_RESET -1 #define OLED_ADDR 0x3C Adafruit_SSD1306 display(SCREEN_WIDTH, SCREEN_HEIGHT, &Wire, OLED_RESET); // ---------- BME280 SPI ---------- Adafruit_BME280 bme(BME_CS); // ---------- Timing ---------- const unsigned long SENSOR_PERIOD_MS  = 1000; const unsigned long DISPLAY_PERIOD_MS = 250; // ---------- Button ---------- const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 30; const unsigned long LONG_MS     = 800; enum BtnEvent { BTN_NONE, BTN_SHORT, BTN_LONG }; struct ButtonState { bool stable = HIGH; bool lastReading = HIGH; unsigned long lastDebounceMs = 0; unsigned long pressStartMs = 0; bool longFired = false; }; ButtonState btn; BtnEvent updateButton(ButtonState &b) { BtnEvent ev = BTN_NONE; bool reading = digitalRead(BTN_PIN); if (reading != b.lastReading) { b.lastDebounceMs = millis(); b.lastReading = reading; } if (millis() - b.lastDebounceMs > DEBOUNCE_MS) { if (reading != b.stable) { b.stable = reading; if (b.stable == LOW) { b.pressStartMs = millis(); b.longFired = false; } else { if (!b.longFired) ev = BTN_SHORT; } } else { if (b.stable == LOW && !b.longFired) { if (millis() - b.pressStartMs > LONG_MS) { b.longFired = true; ev = BTN_LONG; } } } } return ev; } // ---------- Weather data ---------- struct WeatherData { float tRaw = NAN, hRaw = NAN, pRaw = NAN; float t = NAN, h = NAN, p = NAN; float dewPoint = NAN; int trend = 0; }; WeatherData wd; // EMA filter float ema(float prev, float x, float alpha) { if (isnan(prev)) return x; return prev + alpha * (x - prev); } // Dew point (Magnus) float dewPointC(float tempC, float humidity) { const float a = 17.62; const float b = 243.12; float rh = humidity / 100.0; if (rh <= 0.0) rh = 0.0001; float gamma = (a * tempC) / (b + tempC) + log(rh); return (b * gamma) / (a - gamma); } // ---------- PressureTrend ---------- class PressureTrend { public: static const int HIST_LEN = 60; void begin() { for (int i = 0; i < HIST_LEN; i++) hist[i] = NAN; idx = 0; filled = false; lastSampleMs = 0; lastMinuteMs = 0; accum = 0; count = 0; } // call once per loop with latest pressure (hPa) void update(float pHpa) { unsigned long now = millis(); // Accumulate roughly once per second if (now - lastSampleMs >= 1000) { lastSampleMs = now; if (!isnan(pHpa)) { accum += pHpa; count++; } } // Store minute average if (now - lastMinuteMs >= 60000) { lastMinuteMs = now; if (count > 0) { float avg = accum / count; hist[idx] = avg; idx = (idx + 1) % HIST_LEN; if (idx == 0) filled = true; } accum = 0; count = 0; } } // Trend: compare now vs N minutes ago int trendMinutes(int minutesBack, float thresholdHpa) const { if (minutesBack <= 0) return 0; if (!hasAtLeast(minutesBack + 1)) return 0; int current = newestIndex(); int past = (current - minutesBack + HIST_LEN) % HIST_LEN; float pNow = hist[current]; float pPast = hist[past]; if (isnan(pNow) || isnan(pPast)) return 0; float diff = pNow - pPast; if (diff > thresholdHpa) return +1; if (diff < -thresholdHpa) return -1; return 0; } // Accessors for graphing const float* buffer() const { return hist; } int size() const { return HIST_LEN; } // Index of newest valid sample (most recently stored minute) int newestIndex() const { int ni = idx - 1; if (ni < 0) ni += HIST_LEN; return ni; } bool hasAny() const { if (filled) return true; return idx > 0; } private: float hist[HIST_LEN]; int idx = 0; bool filled = false; unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; unsigned long lastMinuteMs = 0; float accum = 0; int count = 0; bool hasAtLeast(int n) const { if (n <= 0) return true; if (filled) return true; return idx >= n; } }; PressureTrend pTrend; // ---------- Min/Max ---------- struct MinMax { float tMin = NAN, tMax = NAN; float hMin = NAN, hMax = NAN; float pMin = NAN, pMax = NAN; }; MinMax mm; void resetMinMax(MinMax &m) { m.tMin = m.tMax = NAN; m.hMin = m.hMax = NAN; m.pMin = m.pMax = NAN; } void updateMinMax(MinMax &m, const WeatherData &w) { if (!isnan(w.t)) { if (isnan(m.tMin) || w.t < m.tMin) m.tMin = w.t; if (isnan(m.tMax) || w.t > m.tMax) m.tMax = w.t; } if (!isnan(w.h)) { if (isnan(m.hMin) || w.h < m.hMin) m.hMin = w.h; if (isnan(m.hMax) || w.h > m.hMax) m.hMax = w.h; } if (!isnan(w.p)) { if (isnan(m.pMin) || w.p < m.pMin) m.pMin = w.p; if (isnan(m.pMax) || w.p > m.pMax) m.pMax = w.p; } } // ---------- Screens ---------- enum ScreenMode { SCREEN_COMFORT, SCREEN_PRESSURE, SCREEN_MINMAX }; ScreenMode screen = SCREEN_COMFORT; // ---------- Alarm ---------- const float HUM_ALARM = 70.0; unsigned long lastBeepMs = 0; // ---------- Display helpers ---------- void drawComfortScreen(const WeatherData &w) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("T:"); display.print(w.t, 1); display.print("C "); display.print("RH:"); display.print(w.h, 0); display.print("%"); display.setCursor(0,16); display.print("DP:"); display.print(w.dewPoint, 1); display.print("C"); display.display(); } void drawMinMaxScreen(const WeatherData &w, const MinMax &m) { (void)w; display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("T "); display.print(m.tMin, 1); display.print("/"); display.print(m.tMax, 1); display.setCursor(0,8); display.print("H "); display.print(m.hMin, 0); display.print("/"); display.print(m.hMax, 0); display.setCursor(0,16); display.print("P "); display.print(m.pMin, 1); display.print("/"); display.print(m.pMax, 1); display.setCursor(0,24); display.print("Hold=reset"); display.display(); } void drawPressureScreen(const WeatherData &w, const PressureTrend &pt) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0,0); display.print("P:"); display.print(w.p, 1); display.print(" "); if (w.trend > 0) display.print("^"); else if (w.trend < 0) display.print("v"); else display.print("-"); display.setCursor(80,0); display.print("60m"); // Sparkline area: bottom half int x0 = 0, y0 = 16, wpx = 128, hpx = 16; if (!pt.hasAny()) { display.setCursor(0,16); display.print("Gathering data..."); display.display(); return; } const float *buf = pt.buffer(); int N = pt.size(); int newest = pt.newestIndex(); // Determine min/max from available points float pMin = 1e9, pMax = -1e9; for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) { int idx = (newest - (N - 1 - i) + N) % N; // oldest->newest float v = buf[idx]; if (isnan(v)) continue; if (v < pMin) pMin = v; if (v > pMax) pMax = v; } if (pMax < pMin) { // all NAN somehow pMin = w.p - 0.5; pMax = w.p + 0.5; } float range = pMax - pMin; if (range < 1.0) { float mid = (pMax + pMin) * 0.5; pMin = mid - 0.5; pMax = mid + 0.5; range = 1.0; } int prevX = -1, prevY = -1; for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) { int idx = (newest - (N - 1 - i) + N) % N; float v = buf[idx]; if (isnan(v)) continue; int x = x0 + (long)i * (wpx - 1) / (N - 1); float norm = (v - pMin) / range; if (norm < 0) norm = 0; if (norm > 1) norm = 1; int y = y0 + (hpx - 1) - (int)round(norm * (hpx - 1)); if (prevX >= 0) display.drawLine(prevX, prevY, x, y, SSD1306_WHITE); prevX = x; prevY = y; } display.display(); } // ---------- Sensor read/update ---------- void readSensors(WeatherData &w) { w.tRaw = bme.readTemperature(); w.hRaw = bme.readHumidity(); w.pRaw = bme.readPressure() / 100.0F; } void updateFilters(WeatherData &w) { w.t = ema(w.t, w.tRaw, 0.2); w.h = ema(w.h, w.hRaw, 0.2); w.p = ema(w.p, w.pRaw, 0.1); } void computeDerived(WeatherData &w) { w.dewPoint = dewPointC(w.t, w.h); } // ---------- Setup/Loop ---------- unsigned long lastSensorMs = 0; unsigned long lastDisplayMs = 0; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); pinMode(BTN_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); pinMode(BUZZ_PIN, OUTPUT); noTone(BUZZ_PIN); if (!display.begin(SSD1306_SWITCHCAPVCC, OLED_ADDR)) { while (1); } display.clearDisplay(); display.setTextSize(1); display.setTextColor(SSD1306_WHITE); display.setCursor(0,0); display.println("Starting..."); display.display(); if (!bme.begin()) { display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.println("BME280 not found"); display.display(); while (1); } pTrend.begin(); resetMinMax(mm); display.clearDisplay(); display.setCursor(0,0); display.println("OK"); display.display(); delay(500); } void loop() { unsigned long now = millis(); // Button events BtnEvent ev = updateButton(btn); if (ev == BTN_SHORT) { if (screen == SCREEN_COMFORT) screen = SCREEN_PRESSURE; else if (screen == SCREEN_PRESSURE) screen = SCREEN_MINMAX; else screen = SCREEN_COMFORT; } else if (ev == BTN_LONG) { resetMinMax(mm); } // Sensor update if (now - lastSensorMs >= SENSOR_PERIOD_MS) { lastSensorMs = now; readSensors(wd); updateFilters(wd); computeDerived(wd); pTrend.update(wd.p); wd.trend = pTrend.trendMinutes(10, 0.5); updateMinMax(mm, wd); // Humidity alarm beep every 5 seconds while above threshold if (!isnan(wd.h) && wd.h > HUM_ALARM) { if (now - lastBeepMs > 5000) { tone(BUZZ_PIN, 2000, 150); lastBeepMs = now; } } } // Display update if (now - lastDisplayMs >= DISPLAY_PERIOD_MS) { lastDisplayMs = now; if (screen == SCREEN_COMFORT) drawComfortScreen(wd); else if (screen == SCREEN_PRESSURE) drawPressureScreen(wd, pTrend); else drawMinMaxScreen(wd, mm); } } Notes/troubleshooting: - If BME280.begin() fails, re-check SPI wiring and that you’re still bypassing the breadboard or have solid connections. - If OLED flickers, reduce DISPLAY_PERIOD_MS to 500ms or avoid clearing as often (more advanced partial redraw). - If humidity reads as NAN, check wiring/power stability. If you paste this into the IDE and it errors, tell me the exact compile error text and what library versions you have installed (or just the names that show up in Library Manager).
My Ender 3 prints have bad stringing between features. What retraction settings should I try?

To dial in stringing on an Ender 3 (Bowden setup), a solid starting point is: - Retraction distance: 5.0 mm - Retraction speed: 40 mm/s - Minimum travel (to trigger retraction): 1.5–2.0 mm - Combing: On (within infill) to reduce unnecessary retractions - Z-hop: Off initially (can add later if you have scarring) But the “right” settings depend a lot on your slicer, filament, nozzle temp, and whether you have any extrusion issues. A few quick questions so we can narrow this down: 1) What slicer and version (Cura, PrusaSlicer, etc.)? 2) Filament type/brand (PLA, PETG) and nozzle temp / bed temp? 3) Retraction currently set to what distance/speed? 4) Any mods (all-metal hotend, different extruder, direct drive conversion)? 5) Do you see blobs/zits at the start/end of moves or just wispy strings?
I’m using Cura 5.4. PLA, Hatchbox PLA. Nozzle 205C, bed 60C. Retraction is 6.5mm at 25mm/s right now. Stock Ender 3 V2, no mods. It’s mostly wispy strings but also little blobs at the ends sometimes.

Good details. For stock Ender 3 V2 Bowden, 6.5 mm isn’t crazy, but 25 mm/s is on the slow side. Slow retraction can let pressure ooze during travel, creating strings and end-of-line blobs. Try this progression (don’t change everything at once; do a stringing tower or a simple two-post test between changes): Step 1: Retraction speed up, keep distance similar - Retraction distance: 6.0–6.5 mm - Retraction speed: 40 mm/s - Prime speed (if Cura separates it): 35–40 mm/s - Enable “Retract at Layer Change”: On - “Wipe Nozzle Between Layers”: Off for now (we can add later) Step 2: Tune temperature Hatchbox PLA often prints clean around 195–205 depending on fan and speed. If you’re stringing at 205, test 200 and 195 as long as you still get good layer bonding. - Run a temp tower or just print your stringing test at 205, 200, 195 (same retraction). Step 3: Reduce distance slightly if blobs appear If you see little “pimples” where travel starts/ends, sometimes too much retraction distance causes a slight delay in re-pressurizing, then it overcompensates and blobs. In that case: - Try 5.5–6.0 mm at 40 mm/s. Also check these Cura settings that heavily affect stringing: - Travel speed: set to 150–200 mm/s (Ender 3 can usually handle 150 comfortably) - “Combing Mode”: “Within Infill” (or “Not in Skin”) to avoid dragging across top surfaces - “Avoid Printed Parts When Traveling”: Off initially (can add after you’ve tuned retraction) - Cooling fan: 100% after layer 3–5 for PLA Before we tune too deep: is your filament dry? PLA can string more when it’s absorbed moisture. Any popping/sizzling sounds during extrusion?
No popping sounds. Filament has been out for a few weeks though. Travel speed is 150. Combing is “All”. Fan is 100% after layer 4. I can try 40mm/s retraction. Should I also turn on coasting?

I’d hold off on coasting until you’ve got baseline retraction + temp good. Coasting can help reduce end-of-line blobs, but it can also cause under-extrusion at the end of walls, especially with Bowden setups. First changes I’d make: 1) Retraction speed: 40 mm/s 2) Combing: change from “All” to “Within Infill” (or “Not in Skin” depending on Cura’s naming in 5.4). Combing “All” can cause long travel moves without retraction, which often increases stringing because the nozzle crosses open areas while still pressurized. 3) Optional: “Max Comb Distance With No Retract”: set to something like 10–30 mm (if available). This prevents Cura from doing extremely long comb moves that ooze. Then re-test at 205. If stringing persists, drop temp to 200. If you still get blobs at travel endpoints, then we’ll consider: - “Wipe Distance” (Cura has “Wipe Nozzle” / “Wipe Distance” in some profiles) - “Outer Wall Wipe Distance” if present - A small amount of coasting (e.g., 0.08–0.2 mm³), but only after retraction is close Do you have a specific test model you’re using (stringing tower, two cylinders, etc.)?
I’m using that common two-tower stringing test, two posts with a bridge between. I’ll try the changes. If I change combing to within infill, will it increase print time a lot?

It can increase print time slightly because you’ll get more retracts and more direct travel moves instead of “combing” along existing paths. But the tradeoff is usually worth it if you’re trying to eliminate stringing and surface scars. With “Within Infill,” Cura will still try to keep travel moves inside infill regions when possible, but it won’t force the nozzle to drag across top surfaces or open air without retracting. On many models, the time increase is modest (often a few percent), but it depends on how “busy” the geometry is. When you do your two-tower test, keep an eye on: - Whether the strings are thin “hair” vs thicker strands - Whether you see small blobs at the start of the second tower (after travel) - Whether the nozzle seems to pause briefly before travel (that can cause a blob) If you can, after you run the next test, tell me: - Retraction distance/speed used - Temp - Whether stringing improved and whether blobs got better/worse
I tried 6.5mm, 40mm/s, combing within infill, temp 205. It’s better, but still some fine hairs. Blobs at tower edges are a bit smaller but still there.

That’s a good sign—now we’re in “fine tuning” territory. Fine hairs can come from slightly too hot, slightly too slow travel, filament moisture, or residual pressure. Next steps (in order): 1) Lower nozzle temp a bit Try 200°C with the same retraction settings. PLA stringing is very temperature-sensitive; 5°C can make a visible difference. If your part still looks strong and not under-extruded, consider 195°C as well. 2) Slightly reduce retraction distance Since blobs are still present, you might be retracting a bit too far and then over-priming or getting pressure fluctuations. Try: - Distance: 6.0 mm - Speed: 40 mm/s 3) Add a small wipe (if available) In Cura, see if you have “Wipe Nozzle Between Layers” or “Wipe Distance.” A small wipe can reduce the “zit” where travel begins/ends by moving the nozzle while retracting/priming. If you only have “Wipe Nozzle Between Layers,” it’s more about seams; still worth trying. 4) Check “Extra Prime Amount” Make sure “Retraction Extra Prime Amount” is 0. If it’s positive, it can cause blobs after retraction. 5) Travel acceleration/jerk (optional) If your printer’s motion settings are conservative, travels can be less crisp, causing more oozing time. But this is a deeper rabbit hole and not always needed. Also: what are your print speeds (outer wall, inner wall)? If you’re printing very slowly at 205, the filament spends more time hot and can ooze more.
Outer wall 25mm/s, inner 50, infill 60. Extra prime is 0. I don’t see wipe distance, only wipe nozzle between layers. Seam is set to “Sharpest corner.” Should I change seam?

Those speeds are reasonable, but 25 mm/s outer wall plus 205°C can be a bit “oozy” on PLA. Lowering temp to 200°C is likely to help. About seam: seam placement mostly affects where the zits accumulate, not whether they exist. If you’re seeing blobs at the ends of features that correlate with seam points, seam strategy matters. “Sharpest corner” is usually good because it hides the seam on a corner, but if the model has lots of corners, you may get many seam events. For troubleshooting, try setting seam to: - “Back” (or “User specified” and pick a consistent location) This doesn’t fix stringing, but it makes it easier to compare results because seam artifacts appear in one place rather than scattered. For the two-tower stringing test, seam isn’t as critical, but for real parts it can help. I’d do this next test: - Temp: 200°C - Retraction: 6.0 mm @ 40 mm/s - Combing: within infill - Travel speed: 150 (leave) - Outer wall: keep 25 for now If you still have fine hairs at 200°C, we can: - Try 195°C (if layer adhesion remains OK) - Consider drying filament (even PLA can benefit after weeks out) - Try increasing travel speed to 180–200 mm/s - Add “Minimum Travel Distance” (so it retracts on shorter travels), but not too low or you’ll get lots of retracts and potential heat creep issues
I ran it at 200C with 6mm retraction. Very little stringing now, just a couple tiny hairs I can wipe off. Blobs are mostly gone. Is it worth trying 195C or should I stop here?

If the print quality is good and layer adhesion is solid, you can stop there—“a couple tiny hairs” is often within normal for PLA, especially on a Bowden machine. Whether to try 195°C depends on your priorities: - If you want maximum cleanliness and the model is mostly cosmetic, 195°C might reduce the last wisps. - If the part needs strength (functional part, load-bearing), staying at 200°C can be safer for layer bonding. A quick practical check: bend a small printed strip or the brim/skirt. If it feels brittle or layers separate easily, you’re too cool. If it’s tough and layers hold, 195 might still be fine. If you do test 195°C, keep everything else identical and look for: - Under-extrusion (gaps in walls) - Rough surface / poor layer bonding - Clicks from extruder (rare at these temps but possible if your flow is tight) One more setting that can help without changing temp: “Enable Retraction” + “Retract at Layer Change” is already on, but check “Minimum Extrusion Distance Window” (if present). Default is usually fine. Do you want settings specifically for Hatchbox PLA overall, or are you also planning to print PETG later (which needs a different approach)?
Mostly PLA. But I do have some PETG I want to try. Also, I’ve heard about calibrating e-steps and flow. Could that affect stringing too?

Yes—e-steps and flow/esteps calibration can indirectly affect stringing and blobs because over-extrusion increases nozzle pressure and ooze. Here’s how they relate: - E-steps (extruder steps/mm) ensures that when you command 100 mm of filament, you actually get 100 mm. If e-steps are off, your flow will be off everywhere. - Flow (extrusion multiplier) corrects for filament diameter variation and the real-world behavior of your hotend/nozzle so walls come out the right thickness. Over-extrusion symptoms that overlap with stringing/blobbing: - Bulging corners - Thick seams - Rough top surfaces - Excess material at line ends A good calibration order is: 1) Mechanical checks (tight belts, stable Bowden couplers, no nozzle partial clog) 2) E-steps (only if you’re comfortable updating firmware or storing settings) 3) Temperature tuning 4) Retraction tuning 5) Flow calibration (single-wall cube) If you want, I can walk you through e-steps and flow for an Ender 3 V2. Quick question: are you comfortable using the printer’s menu to store settings, and do you have calipers?
Yes, I have digital calipers. I can use the menu. I’ve never changed firmware, but I can store settings if it’s in the menu.

Great. On many Ender 3 V2 setups, you can adjust e-steps via the printer menu and store to EEPROM (assuming your firmware supports it). If it doesn’t persist, you may need to add it via G-code start script, but let’s see. E-steps calibration (basic method): 1) Preheat the nozzle to PLA temp (200°C). This reduces back-pressure variability. 2) Remove Bowden tube from hotend (optional but more accurate). If you’re not comfortable disconnecting, you can do it with the tube connected, but results include hotend resistance. - If you do disconnect: release the coupler, pull tube out, and extrude “into air.” Keep filament guided. 3) Mark the filament: - Measure 120 mm from where filament enters the extruder (the entry hole) and make a clear mark with a pen. 4) Using the printer controls, command 100 mm extrusion at a slow speed (e.g., 5 mm/s or 100 mm/min depending on UI). 5) Measure how much filament actually moved: - If the 120 mm mark is now 25 mm from the entry, that means you extruded 95 mm (because 120 - 25 = 95). 6) Compute new e-steps: - New_Esteps = Current_Esteps * (Commanded / Actual) - Example: current 93.0, commanded 100, actual 95 ⇒ new = 93 * (100/95) = 97.89 7) Set the new value on the printer and store settings. Cautions: - Do not extrude too fast with the tube disconnected; keep it slow to avoid chewing filament. - If you leave the Bowden connected, ensure nozzle is hot and not clogged. Can you tell me your current E-steps value (from the menu, often “E steps/mm”)? Many Ender 3 units are around 93.
It says 93 steps/mm.

Perfect. If you’re willing, do the 100 mm extrusion test. If you can, disconnecting the Bowden at the hotend gives a cleaner measurement of extruder-only accuracy. If you’d rather not, do it connected; it’s still helpful. When you’re done, tell me: - How far the mark ended up from the extruder entry (or how many mm actually extruded) Then we’ll calculate the new value.
I did it with the Bowden still connected. Mark at 120mm, extruded 100mm. After extrusion, the mark is 28mm from the extruder entry. So actual extruded is 120-28 = 92mm.

That indicates under-extrusion by about 8%, which is significant. Let’s compute: Current_Esteps = 93.0 Commanded = 100 mm Actual = 92 mm New_Esteps = 93.0 * (100 / 92) = 93.0 * 1.0869565 ≈ 101.09 So set E-steps to about 101.1 steps/mm. A few notes: - Because you did it with the hotend in the loop, this number includes the resistance of your hotend/nozzle. That’s not necessarily “wrong,” but it can slightly overcompensate if there’s extra drag (tight spool, friction in Bowden, partial clog). - If you want the most accurate extruder calibration, repeat with the Bowden disconnected from the hotend. Often the measured under-extrusion improves, and the final value ends up a bit lower than 101. If you keep 101.1: - You may find your flow is now closer to correct, but you should re-check your slicer flow (set it to 100% initially) and then do a single-wall cube to confirm. Do you want to redo the e-steps test with the Bowden disconnected for a cleaner baseline? It’s usually worth it once.
Yeah, I can disconnect it. How do I do that without messing up the tube?

On the Ender 3 V2, the Bowden tube is held by a push-fit coupler at the hotend. To remove it cleanly: 1) Heat the nozzle to ~200°C (important). This softens any molten plastic at the end of the tube. 2) Remove the blue clip (if present) from the coupler. 3) Push the Bowden tube gently further into the coupler (this helps release the teeth). 4) While holding the coupler collar down (press the ring down toward the hotend), pull the Bowden tube up/out. 5) Wipe the end of the tube if it has molten plastic. If it’s deformed or has a “mushroom” shape, you may need to trim 1–2 mm off square with a sharp blade, but try not to shorten it unless necessary. Important: When reinstalling later: - Make sure the tube end is cut perfectly square. - Push it down firmly until it bottoms out against the nozzle/heatbreak (depending on hotend design). A gap can cause clogs and stringing. - Reinsert the clip. For the e-steps test with tube disconnected: - You can extrude filament straight out of the extruder (it’ll curl). Keep it guided so it doesn’t tangle. Do the same mark-at-120mm, extrude 100mm, measure remaining distance. Tell me the new “actual” extruded.
With the tube disconnected, after extruding 100mm, the mark ended up 22mm from the entry. So actual extruded is 98mm.

Much better. Now calculate with the extruder-only measurement: Current_Esteps = 93.0 Commanded = 100 Actual = 98 New_Esteps = 93.0 * (100 / 98) = 93.0 * 1.020408 ≈ 94.90 So set E-steps to 94.9 (round to 95.0 if the menu only allows one decimal or none). That’s a typical adjustment and makes sense for a stock machine. Next: 1) Set E-steps to 94.9 (or 95). 2) Store settings (EEPROM). 3) Reconnect Bowden tube carefully: - Heat nozzle - Push tube fully down - Clip in place After this, keep Cura flow at 100% and do a flow calibration (single-wall cube) to fine-tune. Do you want the quick flow calibration procedure? It’ll help with surface quality and may further reduce residual stringing/blobs.
Yes, walk me through flow calibration in Cura. I’ve seen people do a cube with one wall.

Here’s a straightforward Cura flow calibration using a single-wall cube (often called a “thin wall” test). The goal is to make the printed wall thickness match what Cura expects. What you need: - Calipers - A “single-wall cube” model (20x20x20) or any hollow cube - PLA loaded, nozzle at your tuned temp (start with 200°C) - Your newly calibrated E-steps set Cura setup: 1) Nozzle size: confirm it’s 0.4 mm in Cura. 2) Layer height: 0.2 mm (fine). 3) Wall line count: 1 4) Top layers: 0 5) Bottom layers: 0 (optional; you can keep 2 bottom layers if you want stability, but it slightly affects measurement near the base) 6) Infill: 0% 7) Print speed: moderate (e.g., 40–50 mm/s); outer wall 25 is fine. 8) Flow: 100% 9) Disable “Compensate Wall Overlaps” if you want cleaner measurement (optional; depends on Cura options). Print the cube. Measure: - Let it cool. - Measure wall thickness in multiple spots around the cube, away from corners (corners can be thicker). - Take an average. Expected thickness: - With a 0.4 nozzle and 1 wall, Cura’s default line width is often 0.4 mm (sometimes 0.42). Check Cura “Line Width” setting: if it’s 0.4, expected wall thickness is 0.4. If it’s 0.42, expected is 0.42. Calculate new flow: New_Flow (%) = Current_Flow * (Expected / Measured) Example: - Expected 0.40 - Measured 0.44 - New Flow = 100 * (0.40/0.44) = 90.9% Apply new flow in Cura (material flow) or on the printer (flow rate), but it’s best to do it in the slicer per material. Cautions: - If your wall thickness is inconsistent or wavy, it might be mechanical (loose belts, wobble) rather than flow. - If you have “Horizontal Expansion” or “Hole Horizontal Expansion” set, keep them at 0 for this test. - Make sure you’re not measuring a spot with a seam blob. If you tell me your Cura line width and the average wall thickness you measure, I’ll help compute your flow.
Cura line width is 0.4. I printed it and measured around 0.46 to 0.47 in most places. Average maybe 0.465.

That’s over-extruding a bit (or your line width is effectively wider than 0.4). Let’s compute flow: Expected = 0.40 Measured = 0.465 Current Flow = 100% New Flow = 100 * (0.40 / 0.465) = 86.02% That’s a fairly large reduction. Before you jump straight to 86%, a couple sanity checks: 1) Are you sure the model is truly single-wall? - Wall line count = 1 - Infill 0 - Top/bottom 0 (or minimal) Sometimes Cura will still create extra lines due to “Fill gaps between walls” or because the model’s wall thickness triggers something. In preview, confirm there’s only one perimeter line. 2) Is “Wall Line Width” explicitly 0.4, and not overridden by “Outer Wall Line Width” or “Initial Layer Line Width”? - Initial layer is often wider (e.g., 120%). Don’t measure the first few mm from the bottom. 3) Filament diameter setting: - In Cura, filament diameter should be 1.75 mm. If it’s wrong, flow will be off. If all that checks out, you can try reducing flow, but I’d do it in steps: - Try 92% first, reprint a short section (or a smaller thin-wall test). - Then 88% - Then see where you land Because 86% is unusually low for PLA unless something else is off (like filament diameter set wrong, or line width behavior). What does your filament measure with calipers in a few spots? Many PLA spools are ~1.73–1.76.

Filament diameter measures 1.74 to 1.75 in a few spots. Cura is set to 1.75. Preview shows one wall, but I left bottom layers at 2 so it would stick. I measured higher up. I have “Fill gaps between walls” enabled.

Filament diameter looks fine. “Fill gaps between walls” can sometimes add extra extrusion in places, but if it’s truly one wall, it shouldn’t add a second line—still, it can affect geometry in corners and transitions. Since you measured higher up and preview shows one line, your measured 0.465 suggests Cura is laying down a line wider than 0.4 (or you’re slightly over-extruding). Two approaches: A) Calibrate flow to match actual line width behavior If you want your single-wall to measure 0.4, then yes, you reduce flow. B) Set line width to a more realistic value Many printers with a 0.4 nozzle print better with 0.42–0.45 line width. If Cura is effectively producing ~0.46, you can set line width to 0.45 and then calibrate around that. But typically Cura shouldn’t overshoot that much unless flow is high. I’d do this: 1) Disable “Fill gaps between walls” temporarily (just for calibration). 2) Print the same cube with: - Wall count 1 - Infill 0 - Top 0 - Bottom 2 (fine) - Flow 100 3) Measure again. If it still measures ~0.465, then compute flow. But rather than jump to 86, try 90% first: - New expected if flow 90%: 0.465 * 0.90 = 0.4185 (closer). Then adjust further. So: set flow to 90% and reprint (or print a shorter calibration object like a 20x20x10 cube). Measure again and we’ll converge. Also check if you have “Material Flow” and “Flow” both set (sometimes there’s a global and per-feature). For calibration, keep them consistent.
I disabled fill gaps and reprinted a shorter cube at flow 100. Now the wall measures 0.44 average.

Great—so that setting was affecting the result. Now we calibrate from 0.44: Expected = 0.40 Measured = 0.44 New Flow = 100 * (0.40 / 0.44) = 90.91% So set flow to about 91% for this PLA profile. After setting flow ~91%, you should see: - Cleaner seams - Less corner bulge - Potentially less stringing/blobs because internal pressure is lower Once you do that, it’s worth re-running your stringing test quickly at the settings you liked (200°C, 6 mm @ 40 mm/s) to confirm it didn’t change behavior negatively. It usually improves it. Do you want a recommended baseline Cura profile summary for your Ender 3 V2 PLA now that we’ve tuned e-steps and flow?
Yes, give me a baseline summary. Also, for PETG, what should I change? I know PETG strings a lot.

PLA baseline (Ender 3 V2, Cura 5.4, 0.4 nozzle) — starting point Geometry / quality: - Layer height: 0.2 mm - Line width: 0.40 mm (you can experiment with 0.42 later) - Wall count: 2–3 (2 for normal, 3 for strength) - Top/Bottom: 4–5 layers (0.8–1.0 mm) Temperatures: - Nozzle: 200°C (your tuned value) - Bed: 60°C Cooling: - Fan: 100% after layer 4 - Initial fan: 0–30% depending on adhesion Speeds: - Outer wall: 25 mm/s - Inner wall: 50 mm/s - Infill: 60 mm/s - Travel: 150 mm/s (can try 180 if stable) Retraction (Bowden): - Distance: 6.0 mm - Speed: 40 mm/s - Retract at layer change: On - Combing: Within infill / Not in skin - Extra prime amount: 0 Flow: - Flow: 91% (for this Hatchbox PLA, based on your thin-wall test) - Keep first layer flow at 100% unless you see over-squish Other: - Seam: Back (for consistency) or Sharpest corner (for hiding) - Z-hop: Off initially (add 0.2–0.4 mm only if you get scars) Now PETG changes (big picture) PETG tends to: - Like higher temps (230–245) - Like less cooling (0–40% fan) to maintain layer bonding - String more if too hot or if retraction is too aggressive/fast - Stick very strongly to some surfaces (careful with bare glass) PETG starter settings (stock Bowden Ender 3 V2): Temperatures: - Nozzle: 235°C (start) - Bed: 75–85°C (start 80) Cooling: - Fan: 20–40% after layer 3 (some PETG likes 0–20% for strength; too much fan can reduce adhesion) Speeds: - Outer wall: 20–30 - Inner: 40–50 - Infill: 50–60 Retraction: - Distance: start lower than PLA: 4.0–5.0 mm - Speed: 25–35 mm/s (PETG can be “stringy” if you retract too fast and grind it, but too slow also strings; we tune) Travel: - 150–200 mm/s (fast travels help reduce ooze time) Other PETG-specific tips: - Increase first layer height slightly (0.24) and slow first layer speed. - Use a release layer on glass (glue stick) if you print PETG on glass; PETG can bond too hard and chip glass. - Drying matters more for PETG; moisture makes it string and bubble. When you switch to PETG, don’t reuse the PLA flow number blindly. Do a quick single-wall test for PETG too, because viscosity differs. If you tell me what brand PETG and what build surface you use (glass, PEI, textured), I can tailor the PETG setup.
PETG is Overture. Build surface is the stock glass that came with the Ender 3 V2. I’ve heard PETG can rip chunks out of glass, so I’m nervous.

That’s a valid concern. PETG on bare glass can adhere extremely strongly, sometimes enough to chip the glass when removing prints. The common safe practice is to use a separation layer. Safer PETG-on-glass approach: 1) Clean the glass (for PLA you might use alcohol; for PETG, you still want it clean). 2) Apply a release layer: - A thin layer of glue stick (PVA) is the classic option. - Alternatively, hairspray (less controllable), or a dedicated adhesive like Magigoo (PETG formula). The goal isn’t “more stick,” it’s controlled adhesion and easier release. Removal: - Let the bed cool fully to room temp. PETG often releases easier when cold. - Don’t pry aggressively. If needed, put the whole glass plate in the freezer for 10–15 minutes (if you can remove it safely). Overture PETG starter settings (Ender 3 V2 glass + glue stick): - Nozzle: 235°C (if stringing is heavy, try 230; if under-extruding, try 240) - Bed: 80°C - Fan: 30% after layer 3 (0–20% if you need strength; more fan can reduce stringing but can cause weaker layers) - Retraction: 4.5 mm @ 30 mm/s (start) - Print speeds: outer 25, inner 45, infill 50 - First layer: slow (15–20 mm/s), thicker (0.24), and a bit more squish than PLA but not excessive - Flow: start 100% then calibrate PETG stringing tuning tips: - Reduce nozzle temp first (PETG strings a lot when too hot). - Keep retraction moderate; too high distance can cause clogs with PETG if it pulls molten filament into cooler zones. - Increase travel speed. - Enable “Avoid crossing perimeters” (PrusaSlicer term) / in Cura, “Combing not in skin” helps keep nozzle off visible surfaces. If you want, we can also talk about hardware sanity checks that reduce stringing across all materials—like Bowden coupler play and nozzle condition. How many hours are on your nozzle, and is it still the stock brass 0.4?
It’s the stock nozzle. I’ve printed maybe 2-3 spools through it. I’ve never replaced it. Should I?

After 2–3 spools, a brass nozzle can still be fine with PLA/PETG, but it’s also a common point where quality slowly degrades—especially if any filament had additives (glow, wood, carbon fiber) which are abrasive. Even regular filament can leave residue or slightly wear the orifice. Signs you should replace the nozzle: - Inconsistent extrusion (random under-extrusion) - Rough surfaces that don’t correlate with settings - Increased stringing that tuning doesn’t fix - Nozzle leaks or difficulty cleaning - Your calibrated flow keeps drifting Nozzles are cheap, so replacing it is often a good “reset.” If you do replace: - Use a quality brass 0.4 nozzle (or a hardened steel if you plan abrasives later, but hardened can need slightly higher temps). - Do a proper nozzle change: heat to ~200–230, hold the heater block with a wrench, and swap nozzle carefully. - After changing nozzle, your flow calibration might shift slightly, so re-check your 91% PLA flow. Also check the Bowden couplers: - If the tube can wiggle up/down in the coupler during retractions, it causes inconsistent retraction and stringing. The fix is often replacing couplers or adding clips, and ensuring the tube end is clean and seated. Do you notice any “clicking” during retractions or extrusions, or any visible movement of the Bowden tube at the extruder/hotend during printing?
I do see the tube move a tiny bit at the extruder during retractions. Not huge, but noticeable. No clicking sounds.

That slight tube movement can absolutely contribute to stringing inconsistency because some of your retraction motion is being “absorbed” by the tube shifting rather than pulling filament back consistently. Quick ways to address it: 1) Verify clips are installed on both couplers - The little C-clips (often blue) prevent the coupler collar from moving, which helps lock the tube. - If you don’t have them, they’re worth adding. 2) Check coupler condition - If the teeth in the push-fit coupler are worn, the tube can slip. Replacing the coupler is inexpensive and often improves retraction consistency. 3) Ensure the tube end is cut square and seated - If the tube isn’t fully seated, retractions can cause it to “bounce.” Heat the hotend, reseat the tube firmly, lock with clip. 4) Consider a “Bowden tube fix” at the hotend - Many Ender 3 users install a small printed spacer/washer and use the “hotend fix” to keep the tube pressed firmly against the nozzle to eliminate gaps (reduces clogs and ooze). This is a well-known upgrade, but it’s a bit of a procedure. For the extruder side: - Make sure the extruder coupler is tight and not cracked (the stock plastic extruder arm can crack; Ender 3 V2 sometimes comes with plastic extruder). If it’s plastic, check for hairline cracks near the idler arm pivot. Is your extruder the stock plastic one or a metal extruder?
It’s the stock plastic extruder. I haven’t looked for cracks though.

I’d inspect it closely. The stock plastic extruder arm is a common failure point; cracks can be subtle but cause inconsistent tension, leading to under-extrusion, inconsistent retraction, and stringing/blobs that come and go. How to inspect: - With filament unloaded, look at the extruder arm (the lever you press to insert filament). - Check near the pivot screw and around the area where the spring pushes. - Flex it slightly and look for a crack line. If cracked, replacing with an all-metal extruder (the simple aluminum dual-gear or single-gear upgrade) is a very common reliability upgrade. If you do replace the extruder, you must redo e-steps (because gear diameter/ratio changes). Even if it’s not cracked, adding coupler clips and/or replacing couplers can reduce the Bowden “springiness.” If you want to keep changes minimal: - Add clips first and reseat tube - Retest stringing If you want long-term reliability: - Upgrade extruder + fresh couplers + possibly Capricorn tube (optional) Do you want guidance on reseating the Bowden tube correctly at the hotend to eliminate gaps? That can also reduce oozing and stringing.
Yes, explain reseating it. I’m worried about making a gap and causing clogs.

Reseating the Bowden tube at the hotend (Ender-style) is about ensuring the tube is firmly pressed against the nozzle so molten plastic can’t pool in a gap. Procedure (safe method): 1) Heat the nozzle to printing temp (PLA: ~200°C). This softens any plastic. 2) Remove filament (retract/unload). 3) Remove the Bowden tube from the hotend coupler: - Remove clip - Push tube in slightly - Press coupler collar down - Pull tube out 4) Inspect tube end: - It should be flat and square. - If it’s burnt, mushroomed, or angled, trim 1–3 mm off with a sharp blade or tube cutter. Make the cut as square as possible. 5) Loosen the nozzle slightly (important trick): - With the hotend still hot, use a socket to loosen the nozzle about 1/4 to 1/2 turn (not much). - This creates room so the tube can seat firmly. 6) Insert the Bowden tube: - Push the tube all the way down until it stops. Push firmly. 7) Tighten the nozzle back down while holding the tube down: - This clamps the tube end against the nozzle, minimizing any gap. - Use a wrench to hold the heater block steady so you don’t twist the heatbreak. 8) Install the coupler clip to lock it. Afterwards: - Reload filament and extrude a little to confirm smooth flow. - Watch for any leakage around the heater block (a sign nozzle isn’t sealed). This “loosen nozzle, seat tube, tighten nozzle” method is widely used to eliminate the Bowden gap. If you do this, it can change retraction behavior slightly (usually improves it), so you may find you can reduce retraction distance a bit or get fewer blobs. If you’re not comfortable loosening the nozzle while hot, you can still reseat the tube, but the gap risk is higher. Do you have the tools: a 7mm socket for the nozzle and a small wrench for the heater block?

I have a small wrench and a socket set, I think 7mm is there. I’ll try it later. Back to retraction: what about “retraction minimum travel”? Mine is 1.5mm. Should it be lower to remove those last hairs?

Lowering “Minimum Travel Distance” can reduce hairs in some cases because it forces retraction on shorter moves too. But if you set it too low, you’ll get lots of retracts, which can: - Increase print time - Increase wear on filament (grinding) - Increase risk of heat creep jams on long prints (less common on PLA with good cooling, but possible) - Potentially create more seam artifacts due to frequent pressure changes For Bowden PLA, a reasonable range is: - Minimum travel: 1.0–2.0 mm If you’re already at 1.5 mm and only seeing a couple tiny hairs, I’d leave it. If you want to experiment: - Try 1.0 mm and see if it meaningfully changes anything on a travel-heavy model. - Also consider “Maximum retraction count” and “Minimum extrusion distance window” (Cura has these to limit too many retracts in a small area). Defaults are usually fine. Often the “last hairs” are more impacted by: - Slightly too high temp - Filament moisture - Travel path crossing open space (combing choices) - Nozzle cleanliness (a tiny booger on nozzle tip can drag hairs) A practical technique: clean the nozzle exterior before a print (while hot) with a brass brush carefully (don’t short the heater wires). A dirty nozzle tip can create those wispy hairs even when settings are good. Since you’ve tuned retraction and temp, the next biggest quality boost is usually mechanical consistency (Bowden seating, couplers, extruder integrity) and filament dryness. Do you store filament in open air? If you want, I can suggest low-effort storage/drying options.
I just leave it on the printer. I don’t have a dryer. Is drying PLA really worth it?

For PLA, it depends on your environment. In a dry climate, PLA can sit out for weeks with little change. In a humid environment, PLA can pick up enough moisture to cause: - More stringing and fuzz - Rougher surfaces - Occasional tiny bubbles or “pitting” - Weaker prints Drying is “worth it” if you notice quality drifting over time or if you want consistency. Low-effort options: 1) Simple sealed storage - Large zipper bags or a plastic tote with a gasket - Add desiccant packs (silica gel) This prevents it getting worse, but won’t remove moisture already absorbed quickly. 2) DIY drying - Food dehydrator is the most common and effective. - Some people use an oven, but it’s risky because many ovens overshoot; PLA can deform at relatively low temps. Typical PLA drying: - ~40–45°C for 4–6 hours (varies) PETG: - ~55–65°C for 4–6 hours If you don’t want to buy anything yet, a good first step is sealed storage with desiccant. Then if you still see stringing issues, consider a dryer. Given you’ve already gotten stringing mostly resolved via settings, drying might only give you marginal improvement—nice, but not mandatory. If you start printing PETG, moisture becomes more important; PETG tends to show stringing and popping more readily when damp.
Understood. Another Cura setting: “Enable combing” is within infill now, but I also see “Avoid printed parts when traveling.” Should I enable that?

“Avoid printed parts when traveling” can help prevent the nozzle from crossing over walls/tops and leaving scars or dragging a string across a visible surface. But it can also: - Increase travel distance (more time to ooze) - Create more retractions or longer paths, depending on combing - Sometimes make print time noticeably longer on dense models For stringing specifically, it’s a mixed bag: - It may reduce visible strings on surfaces because travels route around parts. - But longer travels can create more stringing in mid-air if retraction isn’t perfect. Given your stringing is already low, enabling it can improve surface cleanliness on real models. I’d treat it as a “finishing” setting: - Turn it on for cosmetic prints with lots of separated features. - Leave it off for faster prints or when travel optimization is already good. If you enable it, keep an eye on: - Whether print time increases a lot - Whether strings appear between distant features more often Another related setting is “Travel Avoid Distance” (if available). A small value helps. If you tell me what kind of models you’re printing (miniatures, functional brackets, articulated parts), I can suggest whether it’s worth enabling by default.
Mostly functional parts: brackets, enclosures, tool holders. Sometimes small text on top surfaces.

For functional parts and enclosures, I’d prioritize: - Dimensional accuracy - Clean holes - Good top surfaces (especially around text) - Reasonable print time Recommendations: - Keep combing “Within Infill” / “Not in Skin” (good for top surface cleanliness). - Consider enabling “Avoid printed parts when traveling” only if you see nozzle scarring on top surfaces or strings dragged across visible faces. Otherwise leave it off for speed. For small text on top surfaces: - Combing mode “Not in Skin” helps prevent travel lines across the top. - Also consider “Z Seam Alignment” to put seams away from the text area. - Slow down top surface speed (e.g., top/bottom speed 20–25 mm/s). - Make sure flow is tuned (your 91% helps). - If text is small, use 0.12–0.16 layer height and maybe a 0.4 nozzle still works, but a 0.3 nozzle can improve detail (slower though). If you’re printing enclosures: watch for elephant’s foot. Cura has “Initial Layer Horizontal Expansion” which can compensate. But first, ensure first layer isn’t over-squished. Do you have issues with elephant’s foot or holes coming out undersized?
Yeah, holes are always a bit small, and I do get a tiny elephant foot sometimes. I’ve been sanding holes bigger.

Very common on Ender 3-class printers. There are a few levers: Holes undersized: - Partly due to how FDM approximates circles with line segments and how molten plastic pulls inward. - Also influenced by over-extrusion and line width (you’ve already reduced flow, which should help). Fixes in Cura: 1) Hole Horizontal Expansion - This specifically enlarges holes without changing outside dimensions much. - Try +0.1 mm to start (meaning it expands holes by 0.1; Cura’s sign conventions can be confusing—verify with a test). In Cura, there are: - Horizontal Expansion (affects all dimensions) - Hole Horizontal Expansion (affects holes only) Start with Hole Horizontal Expansion if available. 2) Horizontal Expansion (global) - Small negative value can shrink the whole model slightly to compensate for overall “bulge.” - Not ideal if you only care about holes. 3) Print outer walls slower and cooler - Helps accuracy and reduces sag into holes. Your outer 25 is already slow; temp at 200 helps. 4) Increase “Wall Ordering” - Printing inner walls first then outer can improve outer accuracy (Cura typically does inner-then-outer by default). Keep “Outer Wall Last” (again depends on Cura naming). Elephant’s foot: Causes: - Bed too hot - First layer too squished - Not enough cooling early - Over-extrusion on first layer Fixes: 1) Reduce initial layer flow slightly (e.g., 95–98%) while keeping normal flow at 91%. 2) Reduce bed temp after first layer: - First layer bed 60, then 55. 3) Use Cura “Initial Layer Horizontal Expansion”: - Set it to a small negative value, e.g., -0.2 mm. This pulls in the first layer footprint and reduces the lip. 4) Raise Z offset slightly (if you use it), but do this carefully to keep adhesion. A good test is a calibration plate with holes of known sizes and a flat perimeter to check elephant foot. Do you use a probe (BLTouch) or manual leveling?
Manual leveling only. No probe. I use the paper method.

With manual leveling, first-layer squish varies across the bed, which can exaggerate elephant’s foot in some areas. A practical tuning sequence: 1) Level bed carefully, then print a first-layer test (a large square or multiple squares). 2) Adjust the nozzle height so lines are slightly squished but not ridged. You want a smooth surface, not a “plowed field.” Then try these Cura tweaks: - Initial layer flow: 95–98% (since your normal flow is 91%, you might set initial to 95% so it doesn’t under-extrude the first layer) - Bed: 60 first layer, 55 after - Initial layer horizontal expansion: -0.15 to -0.25 mm (start -0.2) This often fixes elephant foot without risking adhesion. For hole sizing: - After you’ve handled elephant foot and flow, try Hole Horizontal Expansion: Start at +0.05 or +0.1 mm and print a hole test. If you want a simple functional approach: design holes slightly oversized in CAD (e.g., +0.2 to +0.3 mm for small holes), but slicer compensation is convenient when printing others’ models. Do you design your own parts in CAD or mostly print existing models?
I design some parts in Fusion 360. For holes I usually add 0.2mm, but it varies.

That’s the right instinct. Hole error depends on: - Diameter (small holes are proportionally worse) - Material and temperature - Printer tuning - Whether the hole is vertical vs horizontal (horizontal holes are a different challenge) A good rule of thumb after tuning: - Vertical holes (printed in XY): oversize by +0.2 mm for ~3–6 mm holes, +0.3 mm for smaller - For press-fit pins, do test coupons But since you’re already calibrating slicer settings, you can make it more consistent: - Keep CAD oversize modest (+0.1–0.2) - Use slicer Hole Horizontal Expansion to dial the rest For horizontal holes (like a hole printed on its side), you may need: - Support tuning - Or design teardrop/diamond holes instead of circles If you want, I can suggest a quick calibration print for holes and elephant foot that you can keep as a reference. It’s usually a 100x100 plate with various hole diameters and a stepped edge.
That would be helpful. Also, I’m curious: does linear advance or pressure advance help with blobs and stringing on these printers?

Calibration print suggestion: - A “hole and tolerance test” plate: - Multiple vertical holes: 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10 mm labeled - A couple rectangular slots - A flat outer perimeter to check elephant foot - Optional: a small text area on top to check top-surface travel behavior You can find models like “tolerance gauge,” “hole test,” or “dimensional calibration” on common model sites. Print it with your normal settings and measure with calipers; it becomes your baseline. Now: Linear Advance / Pressure Advance Yes, pressure advance (Marlin calls it Linear Advance; Klipper calls it Pressure Advance) can significantly reduce: - Blobs at corners - Bulging at line starts/stops - Inconsistent extrusion at speed changes It works by anticipating pressure changes in the nozzle and adjusting extrusion accordingly. However: - Stock Ender 3 V2 firmware may not have Linear Advance enabled. - If you enable it, you need to tune a K-factor. - It doesn’t directly “fix” stringing, but by reducing residual pressure at the end of segments, it can reduce ooze and seam blobs, which can indirectly reduce stringing and zits. If you’re staying stock firmware, you may not have access. If you’re open to firmware upgrades (like a Marlin build that supports Linear Advance), it’s a worthwhile advanced step. Since you’ve already improved stringing with retraction/temp/flow, pressure advance would be the next “quality polish” for seams and corners. Do you know what firmware you’re on (Creality stock, Jyers, MRiscoC, etc.)? The Ender 3 V2 community often uses MRiscoC for better features.
I think it’s stock Creality firmware. I’ve never flashed anything. I’m open to it but nervous about bricking it.

That’s reasonable. Firmware flashing can be safe if you follow the right steps, but it’s not mandatory for good prints. If your main remaining issues are minor, you might not need to touch firmware yet. You can get very good results with: - Correct e-steps - Flow tuned - Retraction tuned - Proper Bowden seating and good couplers - Reasonable temps If you do decide to upgrade later, the typical safe path on an Ender 3 V2 is: - Use a known-good firmware build for your exact mainboard version and display type - Flash via SD card (the V2 is designed for that) - Keep a backup of your settings (esteps, offsets) But that’s a whole separate project. For your current goals—stringing and blobs—you’re already in a good place. If you want one more slicer-based trick for seam blobs without firmware: - “Outer Wall Wipe Distance” (if available) or “Wipe” settings in Cura - “Coasting” very small (0.05–0.15 mm³) - “Reduce infill retraction” off (keep consistent) Since you mentioned you don’t see wipe distance, Cura sometimes hides it depending on profile. You can enable visibility: - In Cura settings search, click the gear icon next to the setting list and enable “Coasting,” “Wipe,” etc. If you want, tell me what profile you started from in Cura (e.g., “Standard Quality 0.2”) and whether you’re using the Ender 3 or Ender 3 V2 machine definition.
I started from the “Standard Quality 0.2” profile for Ender 3 V2 in Cura. I can enable hidden settings. What coasting value would you recommend to start?

For PLA on a Bowden Ender 3 V2, coasting can help reduce tiny end-of-line blobs, but it’s easy to overdo. A conservative starting point: - Enable Coasting: On - Coasting Volume: 0.08 mm³ - Minimum Volume Before Coasting: 0.8 mm³ (or default) - Coasting Speed: leave default (Cura often doesn’t expose) If you see under-extrusion at the end of walls (little gaps), reduce coasting volume to 0.05 or disable it. Also, coasting works best when flow is already close (which you’ve tuned) and when you’re not using too high temp. I’d do a test on a real part with visible seams rather than the two-tower stringing test, because coasting mainly affects wall endings and seams. Alternative to coasting (often safer): “Wipe” (if available) - A small wipe distance (e.g., 0.2–0.4 mm) can reduce the seam zit without reducing extrusion as much as coasting. If you enable coasting, don’t change retraction simultaneously—test one change at a time.
I enabled coasting at 0.08 and printed a small bracket. Seams look a bit cleaner, but I see slight gaps at the end of some outer walls.

That’s the typical coasting tradeoff. Reduce the coasting volume a bit. Try: - Coasting volume: 0.05 mm³ If gaps persist, either reduce further (0.03) or disable coasting and try a wipe-based approach instead. Also check whether the gaps correspond to very short wall segments. Coasting can be too aggressive on small features. Cura has “Minimum Volume Before Coasting”; increasing it prevents coasting on small segments. So you can also: - Increase minimum volume before coasting (e.g., from 0.8 to 1.2 mm³), so coasting only applies on longer lines. A good combo: - Coasting volume 0.05 - Min volume before coasting 1.0–1.2 That often keeps the benefit on long perimeters but avoids starving tiny features.
I’ll try 0.05 and min volume 1.2. Another issue: sometimes I get little strings inside holes or cavities. Is that just unavoidable?

Some internal wisps in cavities can happen, but you can often reduce them. Common causes for strings inside holes/cavities: 1) Travel moves across the void Even with combing, the nozzle sometimes has to cross an opening. If it crosses while slightly oozing, a string forms. 2) Temperature slightly high Even 5°C can be the difference between clean and wispy. 3) Retraction not triggered for short travels If the travel distance is below your minimum travel threshold, Cura might not retract, and you get a tiny string. Mitigations: - Keep combing “Not in Skin” / “Within infill” so it avoids top surfaces, but inside cavities it may still travel. - Lower temp slightly if feasible (you’re at 200; you could test 195 on parts where strength is less critical). - Reduce “Minimum Travel Distance” slightly (from 1.5 to 1.0) if the strings are from very short jumps. - Increase travel speed to reduce time spent oozing during the move. - Enable “Retract at layer change” (you already have). - Make sure “Minimum extrusion distance window” and “Maximum retraction count” aren’t preventing retractions in dense areas. Also, internal strings can be caused by “infill” patterns that create frequent starts/stops. Changing infill pattern (e.g., from grid to gyroid) sometimes changes internal stringing and blobs. If you describe the geometry (like a vertical cylindrical hole, or a rectangular pocket), I can suggest which slicer options help most.
It’s mostly vertical screw holes in brackets, like M3 and M4. I see little hairs inside the holes, and sometimes the hole walls look slightly rough.

For small vertical holes (M3/M4), roughness and internal hairs are common because the printer is constantly making short curved segments with many tiny starts/stops. Try these: 1) Slow down outer wall a bit for small features - You can use Cura’s “Small Feature Speed” setting (if enabled). Set it to maybe 15–20 mm/s. This can improve hole smoothness, though it may increase print time. 2) Enable “Print Thin Walls” cautiously Sometimes helps, sometimes hurts—depends on geometry. For holes, not usually necessary. 3) Ensure “Outer Wall Wipe Distance” or wipe/coasting isn’t starving small segments If coasting is enabled, it can worsen small hole quality. That’s another reason to set a higher “Minimum volume before coasting” so it doesn’t apply on tiny arcs. 4) Hole Horizontal Expansion This helps size, but it can also affect the path planning, sometimes improving smoothness. 5) Consider “Arc Welder” (advanced) Cura outputs lots of tiny segments for circles. If your firmware supports G2/G3 arcs, an “Arc Welder” post-processing plugin can make holes smoother by converting segments into arcs. Stock firmware may not support it, but some do. 6) Slightly lower temp for detail - 195°C can reduce ooze and improve definition on small holes, at the cost of some strength. Also, for M3/M4 holes, many people print them undersized intentionally and then run a drill bit through (or use heat-set inserts). If the hole is for a screw to self-tap into plastic, you want a pilot hole anyway. What’s your goal: clearance holes (screw passes through) or pilot holes (screw bites into plastic)?
Mostly clearance holes for M3 screws, sometimes for heat-set inserts. For inserts I print the hole a bit smaller.

For clearance holes, you’ll get the best results by designing them slightly oversized and/or using slicer hole expansion. Typical clearance hole diameters (rough guides): - M3 clearance: ~3.2–3.4 mm (depends on fit) Given FDM shrink/ovalizing, you might model 3.4 and see if it comes out near 3.2–3.3. For heat-set inserts: - Use the insert manufacturer’s recommended hole diameter and depth, but expect to iterate with a test piece. - Print with slightly higher temp for better layer bonding around the insert area (PLA can work, but PETG is often better for inserts due to higher temperature resistance). - Use more perimeters around the hole (3–4 walls) for strength. - Lower fan a bit can improve strength around insert. If you’re seeing hairs inside clearance holes: - I’d avoid coasting on those features (raise min volume before coasting as planned). - Consider dropping temp to 195 for cosmetic/precision brackets. - Reduce minimum travel to 1.0 only if you confirm the strings are from short moves (you’ll see them mostly on models with lots of tiny separated features).
